claims.claim: PPCM delays the onset of thermal runaway by 277 seconds compared to a cell without PPCM, with TR onset time of 678 s versus 401 s for the bare cell.
claims.quote: the TR onset time of the cell with PPCM was 678 s, which is much longer than that of the cell without PPCM... PPCM could effectively delay the trigger time of TR for 401 s

claims.claim: PPCM increases the peak heat release rate to 29 kW during thermal runaway, which is 3.4 times higher than the bare cell value of 8.52 kW.
claims.quote: the main HRR peak of cell with PPCM reached 29 kW, which was 3.4 times higher than that without PPCM (8.52 kW)

claims.claim: Adding AMTH flame retardant to PPCM reduces the peak heat release rate from 29 kW to 15.5 kW, a 47% reduction, while maintaining TR delay performance with onset time of 652 s.
claims.quote: the cell with PPCM-AMTH showed a peak HRR of 15.5 kW, which is 47% lower than that with PPCM... the onset time of TR for the cell with PPCM-AMTH was 652 s, which is 251 s later than that of the blank one

claims.claim: CPCM promotes rather than delays TR occurrence, with TR onset time of 385 s compared to 401 s for the bare cell, due to its high thermal conductivity of 5.023 W·m−1·K−1 transferring heat back to the cell.
claims.quote: For the cell covered with CPCM, the TR onset time was 385 s, which is shorter than that of the cell without CPCM. It suggests that covering with CPCM promotes rather than delaying the TR occurrence.

claims.claim: Adding APP flame retardant to PPCM reduced peak HRR by only 8% (from 29 kW to 23.9 kW) and accelerated TR onset to 267 s, earlier than even the bare cell, due to char layer increasing heat conductivity.
claims.quote: the peak HRR was 23.9 kW for the cell with PPCM-APP, just an 8% decrease comparing to that of PPCM (29 kW)... the cell with PPCM-APP was the first to go into the TR at 267 s... the char layer generated from APP could be difficult to cover and insulate PPCM

claims.claim: Under heating power of 500 W/m², the onset temperature of thermal runaway for LTO anode cell is 437 K, which is higher than 407 K for graphite anode cell, and the onset time of TR for graphite anode cell is 650 s earlier than for LTO anode cell.
claims.quote: when the heating power is 500 W m−2, the onset temperature of TR for LTO cell is 437 K, which is higher than that for graphite cell (407 K). It can also be found from Fig. 7(a) that the onset time of TR for the cell with graphite anode is 650 s earlier than that of the cells with LTO anode.

claims.claim: The peak temperature of thermal runaway for LTO anode cell (705 K) is much lower than for graphite anode cell (917 K) under the same heating conditions, attributed to the absence of SEI layer in LTO and the lower enthalpy of lithiated LTO compared to lithiated graphite.
claims.quote: the peak temperature of TR for LTO cell is 705 K, which is much lower than that of 917 K for the cell with graphite anode... it is because the reaction between graphite anode and electrolyte releases more enthalpy due to the higher energy density of lithiated graphite compared to the lithiated LTO anode

claims.claim: The thermal stability order of cells with different cathode materials under local heating is: LFP > NCA > NCM > LCO, based on onset temperature and onset time of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the thermal stability order of the LIBs with different cathode materials can be ranked as: LFP cell > NCA cell > NCM cell > LCO cell.

claims.claim: Increasing the separator melting point increases the onset temperature and onset time of thermal runaway, but the improvement in thermal stability becomes less significant when the melting point exceeds a specific value, because side-reactions also contribute heat to trigger TR at higher separator melting points.
claims.quote: the cell separator with high melting point can relieve the occurrence of TR to some extent. However, when the melting point of separator is up to a certain value, the effects of relief may be no longer remarkable... as the melting point of separator increases to a specific value, the heat that triggers TR not only comes from the external heating power, but also heat generation of side-reactions.

claims.claim: When total heating power applied to the cell surface is less than 0.75 W, the cell stays in steady-state and thermal runaway does not occur; the critical total power threshold triggering thermal runaway is between 0.75 W and 1.0 W.
claims.quote: the total power (heating area S × heating power q) applied to the cells of TR is larger than 1.0 W, and the minimum of total power that does not trigger TR is 0.75 W, which means the critical value of the total power that can trigger TR may be between 0.75 W and 1.0 W. In other words, when the total power applied to the cell surface is less than 0.75 W, such heating does not lead the cell into TR

claims.claim: Heating near the bottom of the cylindrical 18650 cell results in earlier thermal runaway onset (200 s at 6000 W/m²) compared to heating at the end (272 s) or middle (536 s), because lower average surface temperature at the bottom position reduces heat loss, increasing temperature rise rate.
claims.quote: the onset time of TR is 200 s, 272 s, 536 s at the position of bottom, end and middle respectively... heating on the bottom of the cell shows lower average surface temperature than heating on the end and middle, which makes the heat loss lower than other heating positions in heating stage. In this case, the temperature rise rate is largest, causing TR to occur sooner than other heating positions.

claims.claim: At ambient temperature above 300 K with heating power of 1000 W/m² and heating size of 15 cm², thermal runaway occurs if air velocity is less than 5 m/s.
claims.quote: When the ambient temperature is larger than 300 K under the heating power of 1000 W m−2 and heating size of 15 cm2, thermal runaway can occur if the air velocity is less than 5 m s−1.

claims.claim: With fixed mini-channel width W = 5 mm, coolant flow velocity u = 0.1 m/s delayed occurrence of TRP but failed to eliminate TRP, u = 0.2 m/s partially inhibited TRP, and u ≥ 0.3 m/s completely suppressed TRP in the three-battery pack.
claims.quote: When using cold plates with fixed W of 5 mm, u = 0.1 m/s delayed occurrence of TRP but failed to eliminate TRP. u = 0.2 m/s partially inhibited TRP, and u ≥ 0.3 m/s completely suppressed TRP.

claims.claim: With fixed coolant flow velocity u = 0.2 m/s, TRP occurred for mini-channel widths W of 3–4 mm, partial TRP occurred for W of 5–6 mm, and no TRP occurred for W ≥ 7 mm.
claims.quote: For fixed u = 0.2 m/s, TRP, partial TRP, and non-TRP occurred in W ranges of 3–4 mm, 5–6 mm, and ≥ 7 mm, respectively.

claims.claim: The critical condition separating TRP and non-TRP regimes for the studied mini-channel cold plate system is described by the correlation u_cri * W_cri^0.9 = 1, where lower critical flow velocity requires larger critical channel width.
claims.quote: ucriW 0.9 cri = 1 ... As expected, lower ucri necessitates larger Wcri and vice versa.

claims.claim: Increasing coolant flow velocity from 0.3 to 0.4 m/s produced little discrepancy in battery temperatures, indicating no further TRP suppression efficiency improvement can be achieved by increasing u beyond 0.3 m/s for W = 5 mm.
claims.quote: When u changes from 0.3 to 0.4 m/s, little discrepancy is found between Fig. 7 (c) and (d), indicating no further cooling performance improvement can be achieved by increasing u.

claims.claim: For prismatic battery packs without liquid cooling, the peak temperature, peak heat release rate, and total released heat of the middle battery (Bat2) were lower than those of the end batteries (Bat1 and Bat3) due to intense heat loss from Bat2 to Bat3 during TRP.
claims.quote: HRRpeak of Bat1 and Bat3 are approximately identical, and both are higher than that of Bat2 ... The significant heat loss from Bat2 to Bat3 also contributes to the lower temperature increasing rate of Bat2.

claims.claim: The conclusions derived from the three-cell prismatic pack model may be invalid for cylindrical cells due to the space among cells, and the cold plate suppression capability may not scale to larger battery modules where lateral heat transfer between cells is significant.
claims.quote: The conclusions derived in this study may be invalid for cylindrical cells due to the space among cells ... in a battery module/pack consisting of much more cells, direct contact of the lateral surfaces of neighboring cells would greatly enhance the heat transfer from high temperature cells undergoing TR to the non-TR ones.

claims.claim: In nail penetration tests with parallel cell short circuits (0.010" nickel), all three packs without PCC propagated fully, while both packs with PCC showed no propagation.
claims.quote: Packs 6e8 did not contain PCC and all three packs propagated without stopping until quenched via the water extinguishing system... Packs 9 and 10 were built with PCC and 0.01000 nickel, and neither pack propagated

claims.claim: PCC reduces the maximum temperature experienced by neighboring cells by 60°C or more in all test conditions.
claims.quote: In all test conditions, the use of PCC lowers the maximum temperature experienced by neighboring cells by 60 °C or more.

claims.claim: In packs without PCC and without parallel short circuits, neighbor cell skin temperatures reached 149–189°C, which is above the 103°C adiabatic thermal runaway onset temperature, yet propagation did not occur consistently due to temperature gradients and short transient durations.
claims.quote: The neighbor cell temperatures T2, T5, and T6 peaked in the range of 149e189 °C before declining... Based on accelerating rate calorimetry data for these cells, temperatures above 103 °C should result in thermal runaway under adiabatic conditions. However, significant temperature gradients across the cells and the short transients spent at these dangerous temperatures can explain why the neighbor cells did not propagate.

claims.claim: In packs with PCC and without parallel short circuits, neighbor cells peaked at 87–108°C and returned below 100°C within 1:26 after trigger, compared to 149–189°C in corresponding packs without PCC.
claims.quote: The neighbor cells showed a slower heating rate than in the packs without PCC and peaked at lower temperatures of 87e108 °C... By 1:26, all neighbor cells returned to below 100 °C.

claims.claim: PCC has a thermal conductivity of 22.0 W/m·K perpendicular to the cell axis, which is approximately 112 times greater than the heat conduction contribution of 0.010" nickel current collectors in the same pack geometry.
claims.quote: Accounting for the nickel at both terminals of the cell can (i.e. e doubling the h), the ratio of Q for a given DT between cells shows that PCC accounts for 112 times more heat conduction than the nickel.

claims.claim: When parallel cell short circuits occurred with PCC present, the additional heat from short-circuit discharges was absorbed by widespread PCC phase change melting, limiting the increase in neighbor cell peak temperatures to only 7–9°C above the no-short-circuit case.
claims.quote: Looking at T2 and T5, the temperature rise was only 7e9 °C higher due to cell short circuits, a curiously small increase given the amount of heat generation expected from fully charged parallel cells... The much larger heat generation from short circuits was absorbed by more widespread PCC melting, storing a large amount of heat during phase change and preventing substantial increases in neighbor cell max temperatures.

claims.claim: Variability in CID activation timing among parallel cells causes irreproducible short-circuit extent and voltage response among replicate packs, even when the eventual propagation outcome is the same.
claims.quote: The CID activation results when a high in-rush of current causes gas generation and internal pressure buildup. The timing of this process would depend on the resistance of the short... We observe that the CIDs activate at irreproducible times, so the extent of short-circuiting varies.

claims.claim: EIPCM with core/matrix ratio of 2:1 (EIPCM3) exhibited a melting temperature of 51.01°C and latent heat of 111.69 kJ/kg, which was the highest encapsulation efficiency (48.74%) among all tested ratios.
claims.quote: EIPCM with the core/matrix ratio of 2:1 exhibited the largest latent heat (111.69 kJ⋅kg−1) and suitable phase change temperature (51.01 ◦C) for battery thermal management applications.

claims.claim: At a 3C discharge rate, EIPCM cooling reduced peak battery module surface temperature from 86.6°C to 66.1°C, a reduction of 23.7%, while keeping the temperature difference within 3°C.
claims.quote: EIPCM exhibited good cooling performance, leading to a 23.7% reduction of the peak battery temperature from 86.6 ◦C to 66.1 ◦C at a high discharge rate of 3C, and the temperature difference was kept within 3 ◦C.

claims.claim: EIPCM delayed the onset of thermal runaway by 495 seconds (from 332 s to 827 s) and reduced the peak battery surface temperature during thermal runaway by 194°C (from 768.8°C to 574.7°C).
claims.quote: the EIPCM could delay the occurrence of TR by 495 s and decrease the peak surface temperature of the battery by 194 ◦C.

claims.claim: Organic PCM (paraffin/EG) ignited after only 5 seconds of flame contact and burned until the paraffin was exhausted, whereas EIPCM did not ignite even after 60 seconds of flame exposure.
claims.quote: the organic PCM was ignited easily after contacting the flame for merely 5 s, and the fire became fiercer until the paraffin ran out. By contrast, the EIPCM did not cause a fire even after igniting for 60 s.

claims.claim: Silica encapsulation reduced the mass loss rate of Na2HPO4·12H2O from 21.63%/min to 1.52%/min after 100°C, indicating significantly improved thermal stability.
claims.quote: The EIPCM sample started to undergo rapid mass loss after 70 ◦C and showed a slower mass loss rate of 1.52%/min after 100 ◦C, which was much lower than that of Na2HPO4⋅12H2O of 21.63%/min.

claims.claim: Silica encapsulation reduced the supercooling degree of Na2HPO4·12H2O from 13°C to 8.5°C by providing heterogeneous nucleation sites that promote crystallization.
claims.quote: with the aid of encapsulation, the supercooling degree of the Na2HPO4⋅12H2O decreased from 13 to 8.5 ◦C. This can be attributed to that the surface of the encapsulation material offers plenty of heterogeneous nucleation sites for hydrated salts.

claims.claim: PCM with high thermal conductivity risks accelerating thermal runaway propagation, making EIPCM's moderate thermal conductivity advantageous for balancing cooling performance and heat propagation blocking.
claims.quote: it has been reported that PCM with high thermal conductivity would take a risk of accelerating TR propagation. Thus, setting EIPCM with not that high thermal conductivity between cells is an effective solution to maintain a balance between cooling performance as well as blocking heat propagation in extreme conditions.

claims.claim: TR propagation time for vertically-arranged batteries (89 s) was faster than for horizontally-arranged batteries (187.5 s) at 2 mm spacing in open space, because the jet fire of battery A directly affected the negative electrode of the adjacent battery in vertical arrangement.
claims.quote: the propagation time Δt was 187.5 s in Case 0h, while that was only 89 s in Case 0v. The main reason was that the jet fire of battery A directly affected the negative electrode of battery C, which caused battery C to heat up instantly and the occurrence of TR.

claims.claim: When battery temperature exceeds 90°C, the SEI film begins to decompose, which is identified as the key node of thermal runaway; if inhibition measures are not taken in time, a series of subsequent chemical reactions will be triggered.
claims.quote: when the temperature of battery is higher than 90°C, the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film begins to decompose, which is the key node of TR. If inhibition measures are not taken in time, a series of subsequent chemical reactions will be triggered, making the battery failed.

claims.claim: Water mist individually applied to vertically-arranged batteries raised adjacent battery C temperature to 136.9°C within only 17.5 s after TR of battery A, exceeding the 90°C SEI decomposition threshold.
claims.quote: in Case 1v, after the TR of battery A, the temperature of battery C reached 136.9°C after only 17.5 s. When the battery temperature was greater than 90°C, SEI films began to decompose.

claims.claim: The barrier efficiency ranking of the three porous materials was: nanoporous aerogel > glass wool > nickel foam; nickel foam (thermal conductivity 3 W/m·K) failed to inhibit TR propagation in horizontally-arranged batteries due to its high thermal conductivity enhancing heat transfer between batteries.
claims.quote: the efficiency of barrier effect is: nanoporous aerogel > glass wool > nickel foam. Since both nanoporous aerogel and glass wool have low thermal conductivity... due to the high thermal conductivity of nickel foam, TR propagation of horizontally-arranged batteries and the safety valve opening of vertically-arranged batteries were caused.

claims.claim: Nanoporous aerogel experienced a sharp increase in thermal conductivity at around 500°C due to breaking of the Si–O–Si grid structure, causing the temperature rise rate of the adjacent battery to increase rapidly before slowing; glass wool is therefore recommended over nanoporous aerogel for large-scale battery packs where higher temperatures may be reached.
claims.quote: the aerogel would cause the grid structure Si–O–Si bond to break at around 500°C, resulting in the disappearance of the SiO2 grid structure and the formation of a single SiO2, which would lead to a sharp increase in the thermal conductivity of aerogel... it is recommended to use glass wool individually for large scale battery packs.

claims.claim: Under synergistic action of porous materials and water mist (Cases 5–7), neither horizontally-arranged battery B nor vertically-arranged battery C underwent TR, and maximum temperatures were kept below 90°C.
claims.quote: Under the porous materials and water mist synergistically inhibit TR propagation, the significant inhibition effects had been achieved in Case 5-Case 7... neither the horizontally-arranged battery B nor the vertically-arranged battery C had TR and the maximum temperatures were below 90°C.

claims.claim: TR occurrence can be effectively controlled only by applying water mist before the battery reaches the critical temperature of TR; water mist must respond within 10 s of TR onset in vertically-arranged batteries to prevent propagation.
claims.quote: The occurrence of TR can be effectively controlled only by applying the water mist before the battery reaches the critical temperature of TR... to avoid the TR propagation, when water mist individually acted on the vertically arranged batteries, it needed to respond within 10 s (the battery C temperature started to sharply in Case 0v) after the TR of battery A.

claims.claim: Thermal conductivity of insulation material impacts TRP suppression performance more greatly than insulation thickness in a 4×4 lithium-ion battery pack.
claims.quote: The results show that thermal conductivity of insulation impacts its performance more greatly than thickness, and glass fiber outperforms silicate and ceramic fibers in preventing TRP.

claims.claim: Glass fiber insulation boards outperform silicate and ceramic fiber boards in preventing thermal runaway propagation, ranked GF > CF > SF.
claims.quote: TRP suppression capabilities of the three insulation materials are ranked as: GF>CF>SF. This phenomenon is primarily attributed to the relatively high thermal conductivity (k) of SF, 0.15 W m−1K−1.

claims.claim: In the temperature range of 300–473 K, thermal conductivity of ceramic fiber varies from 0.075 to 0.08 W m−1K−1 and glass fiber varies from 0.032 to 0.047 W m−1K−1, both much lower than silicate fiber at 0.15 W m−1K−1.
claims.quote: In typical TR induction temperature range of 300–473 K, k of CF and GF vary in the ranges of 0.075–0.08 and 0.032–0.047 W m−1K−1, respectively, much lower than 0.15 W m−1K−1.

claims.claim: When unidirectional insulation boards fail to suppress row-to-row TRP, the total TRP process is accelerated compared to the no-insulation case, because the insulation promotes local heat accumulation in each row.
claims.quote: utilization of unidirectional insulation boards would accelerate TRP in the battery pack if row-to-row TRP cannot be suppressed... Presence of these insulation boards facilities local heat accumulation in each row, promoting in-row TR occurrence.

claims.claim: For bidirectional insulation layout, TRP is only observed for 0.5 mm silicate fiber boards, and the critical heating power triggering TRP is 6.5 kW m−2, much lower than the 20 kW m−2 used in the unidirectional layout cases.
claims.quote: TRP is only observed for 0.5 mm silicate fiber boards, and the critical heating power (6.5 kWm−2) triggering TRP in battery pack is much lower.

claims.claim: For 4 mm thick ceramic fiber boards in unidirectional layout, row-to-row TRP is suppressed and the maximum temperature in Row 2 reaches only 427.31 K, below the 473 K electrolyte decomposition threshold.
claims.quote: the four batteries in Row 1 undergo TR successively but no row-to-row TRP occurs. The maximum temperature in Row 2 is 427.31 K, lower than 473 K.

claims.claim: For 0.5 mm silicate fiber boards in unidirectional layout, the row-to-row TRP times trtr,2−3 and trtr,3−4 are accelerated by 27.2% and 28.7% respectively compared to trtr,1−2.
claims.quote: For 0.5 mm SF boards, the row-to-row TRP times, trtr,2−3 and trtr,3−4, were accelerated by 27.2% and 28.7%, respectively.

claims.claim: The SEI membrane on the negative electrode begins to decompose at 80–120°C, releasing active lithium for an oxidative reaction with the electrolyte.
claims.quote: When the temperature reaches 80–120 °C, the SEI membrane on the negative electrode begins to decompose, releasing active lithium for an oxidative reaction with the electrolyte.

claims.claim: At 150–200°C, the positive electrode material releases oxygen, which reacts violently with the electrolyte, significantly increasing the rate of heat production.
claims.quote: As the temperature further increases to 150–200 °C, the positive electrode material releases oxygen, which reacts violently with the electrolyte, significantly increasing the rate of heat production.

claims.claim: Once battery temperature exceeds 200°C, the electrolyte decomposes and the polyolefin diaphragm melts and shrinks, causing direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes.
claims.quote: Once the temperature exceeds 200 °C, the electrolyte decomposes, and the polyolefin diaphragm melts and shrinks, causing direct contact between the positive and negative electrodes.

claims.claim: The melting points of PP and PE separators are approximately 165°C and 130°C respectively, while a PP/PE/PP composite separator melts at approximately 170°C and a ceramic-coated separator at approximately 200°C.
claims.quote: The melting points of commonly used PP and PE separators are around 165 °C and 130 °C, respectively, while the melting point of a PP/PE/PP composite separator is around 170 °C, and that of a separator with a ceramic coating is around 200 °C.

claims.claim: When SOC exceeded 60% during three-point bending tests on cylindrical LIBs, the battery experienced significant thermal runaway events such as explosions and fire.
claims.quote: Their results showed that when the SOC exceeded 60%, the battery experienced significant thermal runaway events, such as explosions and fire.

claims.claim: When battery temperature exceeds 260°C, fluorinated binders react with intercalated lithium in the negative electrode to produce H2.
claims.quote: When the temperature exceeds 260 °C, fluorinated binders react with the intercalated lithium in the negative electrode to produce H2.

claims.claim: Passive thermal management methods have limited heat dissipation capacity and may not meet heat dissipation requirements for high-power batteries.
claims.quote: Limited heat dissipation capacity; may not be able to meet the heat dissipation requirements for high-power batteries.

claims.claim: Under overcharging conditions, lithium dendrite deposition occurs on the negative electrode and can penetrate the diaphragm, while excessive lithium delithiation on the positive electrode causes structural collapse and increased oxygen release.
claims.quote: Under overcharging conditions, lithium dendrite deposition occurs on the negative electrode, penetrating the diaphragm, while excessive lithium delithiation on the positive electrode causes structural collapse and increased oxygen release.

claims.claim: A 1 mm thick phase change material effectively reduced battery temperature under high discharge conditions.
claims.quote: MoraliU demonstrated that the thickness of the phase change material significantly affects the thermal management system's performance, with a 1 mm thick material effectively reducing battery temperature under high discharge conditions.

claims.claim: In high-power discharge scenarios, a fin spacing of 2–3 mm effectively balances compactness with heat dissipation efficiency and can be combined with phase change materials for improved performance.
claims.quote: in high-power discharge scenarios, a fin spacing of 2–3 mm effectively balances compactness with heat dissipation efficiency and can be combined with phase change materials for improved performance.

claims.claim: Under water cooling at 4C and 5C discharge, the maximum temperature difference (ΔTmax) exceeds 5°C, with values of 5.56°C and 7.74°C respectively at discharge completion.
claims.quote: the ΔTmax usually exceeds 5°C under water cooling, with the values of 5.56°C and 7.74°C at the end of 4C and 5C discharge, respectively

claims.claim: Compared to liquid cooling alone, adding PCM in hybrid cooling reduces ΔTmax by 4.86°C while increasing Tmax by only 0.75°C at 5C discharge completion.
claims.quote: Compared to water cooling, the ΔTmax is decreased by 4.86°C with the composite water and PCM cooling, while the Tmax is only increased by 0.75°C

claims.claim: In a PCM-only cooling system under 1C cyclic operation, all PCM is fully liquefied at 17,240 s, after which battery temperatures rise rapidly due to complete depletion of latent heat storage.
claims.quote: At 17,240 s of 1C cycles and 5880 s of 2C cycles, all PCM is liquefied in PCM-based system, while the battery temperatures begin to rise rapidly since the latent heat storage is totally depleted

claims.claim: At DPCM = 24 mm (2.5 mm PCM thickness, 8.07 mm fin height), Tmax reaches its minimum value of 54.23°C and ΔTmax is reduced to 3.10°C during 5C discharge at 40°C ambient.
claims.quote: When the DPCM increases to 24 mm... the ΔTmax is slightly decreased to 3.10°C, and Tmax is decreased to the minimum value of 54.23°C

claims.claim: Adding 16 wt% EG to paraffin PCM increases thermal conductivity by 14.5 times, reduces latent heat by 16.00%, and reduces specific heat by 9.85% compared to pure paraffin.
claims.quote: When the ωEG is increased from 0 to 16%, the thermal conductivity of CPCM is increased to 14.5 times, the latent heat is reduced by 16.00%, and the specific heat is reduced by 9.85%

claims.claim: At 16 wt% EG in CPCM, battery Tmax is decreased by 7.45°C to 46.78°C compared to pure PCM, while ΔTmax is 4.30°C at 5C discharge completion.
claims.quote: Compared to pure PCM, the Tmax is decreased by 7.45°C to the minimum value of 46.78°C at ωEG = 16%, while ΔTmax is 4.30°C

claims.claim: When EG mass fraction exceeds 16 wt%, the severe reduction in latent heat prevents CPCM near the battery from completely absorbing generated heat, causing a slight increase in Tmax.
claims.quote: as the ωEG exceeds 16%, the severe reduction in latent heat prevents the CPCM close to the battery from completely absorbing the generated heat, resulting in a slight increase in Tmax

claims.claim: The thermal resistance between PCM and the Al cold plate is reduced by 98.39% by combining protruding fins and 16 wt% EG CPCM, reducing total thermal resistance from batteries to cooling water by 88.36%.
claims.quote: the greatest enhancement of heat transfer is between the PCM and the Al plate, with a 98.39% reduction in thermal resistance... the total thermal resistance from batteries to cooling water in hybrid BTMS is reduced by 88.36% after structural and material optimization

claims.claim: Using a fixed 1C charge or fixed 1C discharge cycle strategy keeps battery Tmax below 47.22°C and ΔTmax below 4.69°C during continuous high-rate cyclic operations in the optimized hybrid BTMS.
claims.quote: using the matching strategy with a fixed charge or discharge rate of 1C provides lower temperatures and better temperature uniformity, which can always control the temperatures below 47.22°C and temperature difference below 4.69°C

claims.claim: The maximum operating temperature of Li-ion batteries is identified to be 40°C from the perspective of battery performance, battery life, and thermal safety.
claims.quote: The maximum operating temperature of Li-ion batteries (LIBs) is identified to be 40 ◦C from the perspective of battery performance, battery life and thermal safety

claims.claim: PCMs, whether organic or inorganic, offer very low thermal conductivity of nearly 0.5 W/m K, which prevents uniform diffusion of heat in the PCM matrix.
claims.quote: the PCMs, either organic or inorganic, offer very low thermal conductivity (nearly 0.5 W∕m K). The low thermal conductivity prevents the uniform diffusion of heat in the PCM matrix.

claims.claim: A higher TCE volume fraction promotes improved heat conduction and a more uniform temperature profile during phase change but leads to a faster transition to higher temperatures once the PCM is fully melted.
claims.quote: a higher TCE fraction promotes improved heat conduction and a more uniform temperature profile during phase change but leads to a faster transition to higher temperatures once the PCM is fully melted

claims.claim: The TCE volume fraction exhibits a non-monotonic effect on operating time, with the maximum operating time achieved at a volume fraction of 0.1 among the tested cases (0.05 to 0.3).
claims.quote: The results reveal a non-monotonic trend, where the operating time initially increases with 𝜙 before decreasing... In this study, 𝜙 = 0.1 yields the longest operating time among all tested cases.

claims.claim: Increasing the filter radius (Rmin) from 0.5 mm to 0.75 mm reduces operating time by 150 s, a larger reduction than the 60 s reduction observed when increasing from 0.25 mm to 0.5 mm.
claims.quote: increasing 𝑅𝑚𝑖𝑛 from 0.25 mm to 0.5 mm reduces the operating time by 60 s, while a further increase from 0.5 mm to 0.75 mm leads to a more significant reduction of 150 s

claims.claim: For every 1 mm increase in the inner shell radius, the operating time extends by approximately 157 s, indicating a nearly linear relationship between inner shell radius and operating time.
claims.quote: for every 1 mm increase in the inner shell radius, the operating time extends by approximately 157 s, demonstrating the strong influence of 𝑅shell,in on the system's thermal performance

claims.claim: The topology-optimized BTMS reaches 40°C after 700 s, whereas straight, ring + straight, and X-shaped BTMSs reach the same temperature in 450 s, 530 s, and 460 s, respectively, corresponding to 55%, 32%, and 52% longer operating times for the topology-optimized design.
claims.quote: The topology-optimized BTMS reaches 40 ◦C after 700 s, whereas the straight, ring + straight, and X-shaped BTMSs reach the same temperature in just 450 s, 530 s, and 460 s, respectively. This indicates that the topology-optimized design achieves 55%, 32%, and 52% longer operating times

claims.claim: The maximum temperature non-uniformity in the PCM region during melting for the topology-optimized BTMS is 11.2°C, compared to 14.6°C, 17.5°C, and 14.6°C for straight, ring + straight, and X-shaped designs, respectively.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature non-uniformity (𝛥𝑇PCM) during the PCM melting phase for the topology-optimized, straight, ring + straight, and X-shaped BTMS designs are 11.2 ◦C, 14.6 ◦C, 17.5 ◦C, and 14.6 ◦C, respectively.

claims.claim: PCM selection has minimal influence on thermal performance of topology-optimized BTMS, as operating times remain nearly identical across eicosane, docosane, and tetracosane, attributable to similarities in their thermophysical properties.
claims.quote: the topology optimized structures of the TCE show minimal variation with change in PCMs. This can be attributed to the similarities in thermophysical properties among the chosen PCMs... the performance of these structures is largely independent of the choice of PCM.

claims.claim: Increasing the number of cooling units from 1 to 3 reduces the maximum temperature difference by 17.1 K, but further increasing from 3 to 5 units only slightly improves this parameter by 1.3 K.
claims.quote: increasing the number of cooling units from 1 to 3 reduces the maximum temperature difference by 17.1 K, but further increasing from 3 to 5 units only slightly improves this parameter by 1.3 K

claims.claim: Compared to deionized-water, engine-oil, and ethylene-glycol, sCO2 increases the heat transfer coefficient by factors of 2.69, 9.97, and 6.11, respectively, while reducing the pressure drop by factors of 1.47, 4.58, and 14.52, respectively.
claims.quote: It increases the heat transfer coefficient by factors of 2.69, 9.97, and 6.11, respectively, while reducing the pressure drop by factors of 1.47, 4.58, and 14.52, respectively

claims.claim: sCO2 decreases the maximum battery module temperature by 4.39 K, 13.45 K, and 9.55 K compared to deionized-water, engine-oil, and ethylene-glycol, respectively.
claims.quote: sCO2 decreases the maximum temperature by 4.39 K, 13.45 K, and 9.55 K compared to deionized-water, engine-oil, and ethylene-glycol, respectively

claims.claim: At operating pressure of 8 MPa with inlet temperature of 308.15 K, the sCO2 specific heat capacity is approximately 4.84 times greater than at 7.5 MPa and 2.79 times greater than at 8.5 MPa, because the inlet temperature is proximate to the pseudo-critical temperature of 307.9 K at 8 MPa.
claims.quote: at p = 8 MPa, the heat capacity is approximately 4.84 times greater than at p = 7.5 MPa and 2.79 times greater than at p = 8.5 MPa

claims.claim: A Richardson number exceeding 0.01 indicates significant buoyancy forces; for sCO2 in cooling channels, Richardson numbers of 0.0033, 0.0130, and 0.0367 are observed for channel diameters of 3, 4, and 5 mm respectively, indicating significant buoyancy effects at d = 4 and 5 mm.
claims.quote: a Richardson number exceeding 0.01, indicates significant buoyancy forces. In our study, Richardson numbers of 0.0033, 0.0130, and 0.0367 are observed for d = 3, 4, and 5 mm, respectively

claims.claim: The sCO2-based BTMS eliminates the need for a coolant temperature recovery system because sCO2's high heat capacity results in temperature increases of less than 0.5 K between inlet and outlet, enabling temperature recovery via free or natural cooling.
claims.quote: sCO2's high heat capacity minimizes temperature differences between its inlet and outlet, with observed temperature increases of less than 0.5 K. This facilitates the temperature recovery process through free or natural cooling methods

claims.claim: The temperature difference within each Li-Ion cell in a BTMS with 1 cooling unit is approximately 20 K, whereas the recommendation is that this difference should not exceed 5 K for optimal lifespan.
claims.quote: the temperature difference within each cell in this scenario is approximately 20 K, whereas the recommendation is that this difference should not exceed 5 K for optimal lifespan

claims.claim: PCM cooling provides up to 15 °C lower maximum battery temperatures compared to air cooling, but only as long as the PCM remains solid (not fully melted).
claims.quote: PCM cooling outperforms air cooling, providing up to 15 °C lower maximum battery temperatures as long as PCM remains solid.

claims.claim: After complete PCM melting, battery temperature with PCM cooling surpasses the temperature achieved through air cooling, because molten stationary PCM has a lower heat transfer coefficient than air.
claims.quote: Since the molten PCM remains stationary around the battery, its heat transfer coefficient is much lower than that of air. Consequently, after complete PCM melting, the battery temperature surpasses the level achievable with air cooling.

claims.claim: An optimal configuration of 6 copper fins with 11.67 mm height and 0.2 mm thickness provided 8 °C lower peak temperatures than without fins over 2 hours.
claims.quote: An optimal configuration of 6 copper fins (385 W/m.K) with 11.67 mm height and 0.2 mm thickness provided 8 °C lower peak temperatures than without fins over 2 h.

claims.claim: Reducing fin thickness below 0.2 mm caused excessive PCM melting between fins, resulting in over 5 °C higher peak temperatures than the 0.2 mm case.
claims.quote: fin thicknesses below 0.2 mm caused excessive melting between fins, degrading performance with over 5 °C higher peak temperatures than the 0.2 mm case.

claims.claim: For longitudinal fins, a 4-fin configuration with 10 mm height outperformed 10 fins of 3 mm height by over 5 °C in peak battery temperature.
claims.quote: For longitudinal fins, a 4-fin configuration with 10 mm height outperformed 10 fins of 3 mm height by over 5 °C.

claims.claim: With high conductivity PCM such as paraffin wax (0.242 W/m·K), varying fin geometry had minimal impact on peak temperatures, which remained within 1 °C.
claims.quote: With high conductivity PCMs like paraffin wax (0.242 W/m.K), varying fin geometry had minimal impact on peak temperatures, which remained within 1 °C.

claims.claim: Increasing the number of copper fins beyond an optimal value reduces cooling performance because less PCM volume is accommodated between fins, and excessive fins form a thick metallic shell that acts as an insulator.
claims.quote: As the number of fins increases, less PCM is accommodated between the copper fins... if the number of fins increases excessively, a thick metallic shell made of copper forms around the battery, acting as an insulator and causing an increase in the maximum battery temperature.

claims.claim: Copper fins outperformed aluminum fins by 2–3 °C in peak battery temperature reduction due to higher thermal conductivity, but copper has approximately 3.3 times higher density than aluminum (8933 vs 2719 kg/m³).
claims.quote: Copper fins outperformed aluminum (237 W/m K) by 2–3 °C due to higher conductivity, though aluminum's lower density (2719 vs 8933 kg/m3) is an advantage.

claims.claim: In nail penetration tests on coin cells, approximately 4 wt. % pentadecane reduced the maximum temperature increase by approximately 60%, from approximately 75°C in the reference cell to approximately 30°C.
claims.quote: The addition of pentadecane reduces DTmax by ~60%, from ~75°C in the reference cell to ~30°C.

claims.claim: In impact tests on pouch cells, approximately 5 wt. % pentadecane reduced the maximum temperature increase by nearly an order of magnitude, to only 5°C.
claims.quote: The maximum increase in temperature of the pouch cell embedded with TRR is only 5°C, order-of-magnitude lower than that of the reference cell.

claims.claim: Addition of 4 wt. % pentadecane increases both series resistance and charge transfer resistance by approximately 50 times, with Rct increasing from 375.2 Ω to 20004.3 Ω.
claims.quote: the charge transfer resistance (Rct) for reference and pentadecane-modiﬁed cells are 375.2 Ω and 20004.3 Ω, respectively. Both Rs and Rct increase by ~50 times with the addition of pentadecane.

claims.claim: Pentadecane suppresses lithium ion transport by forming a physical blocking layer in the separator due to its immiscibility with electrolyte and superior wettability to the separator material.
claims.quote: Since pentadecane is immiscible with the electrolyte and is more wettable to the separator, it will repel electrolyte and form a physical blocking layer in the separator, which suppresses Li+ transport.

claims.claim: Under 1 kPa compression, pentadecane diffuses approximately 8.7 mm in the electrode layer stack in 15 seconds.
claims.quote: When the electrode stack is compressed at 1 kPa, pentadecane could travel 8.7 mm on separator in 15 s.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in LIB occurs when temperature rises above 90°C, triggering a series of exothermic electrochemical reactions and chemical decompositions.
claims.quote: When the temperature rises to above 90°C, a series of exothermic electrochemical reactions and chemical decompositions takes place, and thermal runaway occurs.

claims.claim: Octane has lower thermal-runaway mitigation efficiency than pentadecane, possibly because its low boiling point of approximately 125°C causes it to evaporate after nail penetration as cell temperature rapidly increases.
claims.quote: Octane has a lower efﬁciency, possibly due to its low boiling point ~125°C. After nail penetration, the temperature inside the LIB cell increases rapidly and causes evaporation of octane.

claims.claim: Fire incidents during charging sessions account for approximately 26% of all fire cases in new energy vehicles, attributed to direct battery ignition, charging equipment malfunctions, and overcharging.
claims.quote: Statistical analysis reveals that fire incidents during charging sessions account for approximately 26% of all cases, attributed to direct battery ignition, charging equipment malfunctions, and overcharging.

claims.claim: Fires occurring while vehicles are parked represent approximately 13% of fire incidents, particularly in climates with considerable day-to-night temperature variations.
claims.quote: fires occurring while vehicles are parked represent a significant portion (about 13%) of incidents, particularly in climates with considerable day-to-night temperature variations

claims.claim: The first stage of thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries is decomposition of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film on the negative electrode surface when battery temperature exceeds the normal working range.
claims.quote: the first step of thermal runaway is that when the temperature of the lithium battery exceeds the normal working range, the solid electrolyte interface film (SEI) on the surface of the negative electrode will decompose

claims.claim: When the diaphragm melts during thermal runaway, the positive and negative electrodes short-circuit, accelerating thermal runaway, and electrolyte decomposition produces combustible gas.
claims.quote: When the diaphragm melts, the positive and negative electrodes short-circuit, accelerating the thermal runaway, and the electrolyte decompositions to produce combustible gas and burn

claims.claim: Greater overcharge current results in shorter thermal runaway time, greater critical temperature, greater regional temperature differential, and greater difference in temperature between interior and exterior of the battery.
claims.quote: The greater the overcharge current, the shorter the thermal runaway time, the greater the critical temperature, the greater the regional temperature differential, and the greater the difference in temperature between the interior and outside.

claims.claim: Decreasing battery thickness can effectively strengthen heat dissipation ability and reduce internal temperature rise heat production rate.
claims.quote: He found that decreasing the thickness can effectively strengthen the heat dissipation ability and reduce the internal temperature rise heat production rate.

claims.claim: During over-discharge, copper collector dissolves into divalent copper ions at the negative electrode, which migrate through the electrolyte to the positive electrode forming copper ion dendrites and causing positive-negative electrode short circuit leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: After the battery reaches the minimum discharge voltage, it will dissolve into divalent copper ions in the negative electrode collector. These ions will then pass through the electrolyte to reach the positive electrode, which has a lower potential. This will result in the formation of copper ion dendrites and the shorting of the positive and negative electrodes, which will cause a thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Internal short circuits between negative electrode material and aluminum collector have low short circuit resistance and poor thermal conductivity, making thermal runaway highly likely.
claims.quote: The first category of internal short circuits has a low short circuit resistance value and poor thermal conductivity, making thermal runaway highly likely.

claims.claim: Aligned cell arrangement in air-cooled battery packs has 26.1% higher cooling efficiency and better thermal uniformity than staggered arrangement.
claims.quote: Yang et al. investigated the effect of different arrangements of cells on the temperature and showed that aligned arrangement has 26.1% higher cooling efficiency and better thermal uniformity than staggered arrangement

claims.claim: Multi-objective optimization of inlet and outlet heights and battery spacing reduced maximum battery pack temperature by 9.55% and temperature difference by 25.89% compared to pre-optimization.
claims.quote: Li et al. used a multi-objective optimization method with orthogonal experiments, which converged after many iterations to arrive at the optimal solution for the inlet and outlet heights and battery spacing, which reduced the maximum temperature of the battery pack by 9.55% and the temperature difference by 25.89% compared to the pre-optimization period

claims.claim: A stepped heat pipe-phase change material cooling device reduced temperature standard deviation from 5.2°C to 2.4°C in simulation and from 4.7°C to 2.3°C in real experiment for a 1P11S battery pack.
claims.quote: The findings demonstrate a smoother temperature change curve, a decrease in the simulation experiment's temperature standard deviation from 5.2 °C to 2.4 °C, and a reduction in the real experiment's temperature standard deviation from 4.7 °C to 2.3 °C

claims.claim: When the number of flow channels in a cooling plate for 21700 lithium-ion battery modules is too low or too high, the temperature control effect is negatively affected; an 8-channel structure achieves the lowest overall average battery temperature.
claims.quote: too low or too high flow channels for 21700 lithium-ion battery modules would affect the temperature control effect. Only when the number of flow channels in the cooling plate is controlled within a certain number can the overall temperature control effect be achieved. The 8-channel structure can effectively control the operating temperature, and the overall average temperature of the battery is the lowest

claims.claim: The current collector accounts for more than 90% of the electric conductivity and approximately 90% of the mechanical strength of the electrode in a lithium-ion battery.
claims.quote: current collector accounts for more than 90% of the electric conductivity and ~90% of the mechanical strength of the electrode

claims.claim: When local temperature at an internal shorting site reaches approximately 110°C, secondary reactions including decomposition of electrolyte and electrodes are activated.
claims.quote: When T reaches ~110°C, a number of secondary reactions would be activated, including the decomposition of electrolyte and electrodes.

claims.claim: With a heterogeneous current collector, the temperature increase caused by internal shorting after mechanical impact is approximately 30% of the reference cell peak temperature increase, comparable to a fully discharged cell.
claims.quote: The increase in the resulting temperature, DT, is around 30% of DTref, close to that of a fully discharged half-cell, suggesting that the heat generation associated with internal shorting is negligible.

claims.claim: After impact, a reference cell with smooth current collector showed residual voltage of 2.00 ± 0.4 V, while a cell with heterogeneous current collector showed residual voltage of 4.54 ± 0.1 V.
claims.quote: if the cathode is based on smooth current collector, after impact, the residual voltage is 2.00 ± 0.4 V; if the cathode is based on heterogeneous current collector, the residual voltage is 4.54 ± 0.1 V

claims.claim: The thermally triggered mitigation mechanisms such as PTC additives and low-melting-point separators have triggering temperatures of 110–130°C, which is already at the critical point of thermal runaway (~120°C).
claims.quote: The triggering temperature must be much higher than the operation/storage temperature range of LIB, around 110–130°C, which is already at the critical point of thermal runaway (~120°C).

claims.claim: The critical oven temperature to trigger thermal runaway is approximately 175°C for the LiCoO2 cell, 195°C for the LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 cell, 255°C for the Li1.1(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)0.9O2 cell, and 275°C for the LiMn2O4 cell.
claims.quote: the critical oven temperatures to trigger thermal runaway are about 175°C for the LiCoO2 cell, 195°C for the LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 cell, 255°C for the Li1.1(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)0.9O2 cell and 275°C for the LiMn2O4 cell.

claims.claim: For the LiFePO4 cell under oven abuse, the maximum temperature rise caused by exothermic reactions does not exceed 50°C, and no thermal runaway occurs.
claims.quote: As for the LiFePO4 cell, the maximum temperature rise caused by the exothermic reactions will not be greater than 50°C during oven abuse processes, indicating it is theoretically invulnerable to thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The onset temperatures of positive-solvent reaction are 175°C for LiCoO2, 200°C for LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2, 255°C for Li1.1(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)0.9O2, 270°C for LiMn2O4, and 260°C for LiFePO4.
claims.quote: the onset temperatures of positive-solvent reaction for different cathode materials are different, which are 175°C, 200°C, 255°C, 270°C, and 260°C for LiCoO2, LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2, Li1.1(Ni1/3Co1/3Mn1/3)0.9O2, LiMn2O4, and LiFePO4 cells, respectively.

claims.claim: At completion of the positive-solvent reaction, LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 produces the highest temperature rise (~210°C) while LiFePO4 produces the lowest (~15°C) among the five cathode materials studied.
claims.quote: at the completion of the positive-solvent reaction (a = 1), the LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 cell has the highest temperature rise of about 210°C and the LiFePO4 has the lowest temperature rise of only about 15°C.

claims.claim: The positive-solvent reaction is identified as the main heat generation source during thermal runaway processes for all five batteries with differing cathode materials.
claims.quote: the positive-solvent reaction is the main heat generation source during thermal runaway processes for all the five batteries of differing cathode materials.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition in anode-electrolyte configurations consistently initiates near 100°C with minimal heat release.
claims.quote: The SEI (solid electrolyte interphase) decomposition in A.+E. configurations consistently initiates near 100°C with minimal heat release.

claims.claim: Cathode decomposition in cathode-electrolyte configurations occurs around 200°C, releasing substantial heat and oxygen.
claims.quote: Cathode decomposition in C.+E. occurs around 200°C, releasing substantial heat and oxygen—acting effectively as an oxidizer source.

claims.claim: In high-Ni configurations (80%- and 90%-Ni), accelerated lattice oxygen release compresses the thermal gap between cathode decomposition and thermal runaway, resulting in a merged heat-release event.
claims.quote: the enhanced kinetic propensity for exothermic electrolyte oxidation, triggered by the accelerated release of lattice oxygen in high-Ni configurations (80%- and 90%-Ni), compresses the thermal gap between cathode decomposition and thermal runaway, resulting in a merged heat-release event.

claims.claim: Ni60 (60%-Ni) maintains distinct decomposition and thermal runaway peaks due to reduced kinetic propensity, whereas Ni80 and Ni90 show merged peaks.
claims.quote: In contrast, compositions with reduced kinetic propensity (60%-Ni) maintain distinct decomposition and thermal runaway peaks.

claims.claim: Stabilizing dopants (cobalt, manganese, aluminum) mitigate combustion hazard by suppressing oxygen evolution and elevating decomposition thresholds in nickel-rich cathodes.
claims.quote: stabilizing dopants (cobalt, manganese, aluminum) mitigate this combustion hazard by suppressing oxygen evolution and elevating decomposition thresholds.

claims.claim: Ni80 exhibited the highest probability of thermal runaway among compositions studied, despite having lower Ni content than Ni90, attributed to insufficient Al content that failed to suppress oxygen evolution and electrolyte oxidation.
claims.quote: the Al content in Ni80 was insufficient to maintain these stabilizing effects and preserve the integrity of the layered lattice. This deficiency facilitated oxygen evolution and accelerated electrolyte oxidation during heating, outweighing the potential stability gains provided by the lower Ni content and the presence of Mn. Consequently, Ni80 exhibited the most pronounced exothermic behavior and the highest probability of TR among the compositions studied.

claims.claim: Ni80 and Ni90 exceeded the thermal runaway self-heating rate threshold of 1000°C/min, with maximum self-heating rates of 1731.88 and 1489.836°C/min respectively, while Ni60 reached only 208.61°C/min.
claims.quote: Ni80 and Ni90 exhibited maximum self-heating rates of 1731.88 and 1489.836°C/min, respectively, both exceeding the threshold. In contrast, Ni60 showed a significantly lower maximum self-heating rate of 208.61°C/min, well below the threshold.

claims.claim: Ni60 initiated thermal runaway at an onset temperature of 264.29°C and peak of 288.38°C, while Ni80 had onset at 197.33°C and peak at 217.96°C, and Ni90 had onset at 184.74°C and peak at 205.02°C.
claims.quote: Ni60 initiated TR and reached its peak at the highest temperature among the 3 samples. On the other hand, Ni80 had an onset temperature of 197.33°C and a peak temperature of 217.96°C.

claims.claim: The activation energy of SEI decomposition serves as a quantitative proxy for electrolyte reactivity because all samples use the same graphite-based anode, making differences in SEI decomposition kinetics solely attributable to electrolyte differences.
claims.quote: Given the uniform use of graphite-based anodes, the activation energy of SEI decomposition served as a quantitative measure of electrolyte reactivity: Ni60 cells showed the highest activation energy (lowest reactivity), whereas Ni80 and Ni90 cells exhibited lower activation energies, indicating more reactive electrolytes.

claims.claim: Ni80 released over 900 J/g of heat during thermal runaway, sufficient to trigger thermal runaway in surrounding cells, whereas Ni60 generated insufficient heat for propagation.
claims.quote: Ni80 released over 900 J/g of heat during TR, making it sufficient to trigger TR in surrounding cells.

claims.claim: The onset temperature of exothermic reactions (TOER) of LIBs decreases to as low as 31.7°C at 165% SOC, due to internal short-circuit caused by separator piercing by lithium dendrites.
claims.quote: The onset temperature of exothermic reactions of LIBs decreases with the increase of state of charge (SOC) - to as low as 31.7°C at 165% SOC - due to internal short-circuit caused by separator piercing.

claims.claim: At 100% and 120% SOC, the cathode contributes approximately 80% or more of the total heat during thermal runaway, with the anode contributing the remainder.
claims.quote: When the LIBs were at 100% and 120% SOC, the heat contribution ratio from the cathode was the majority (≥80%).

claims.claim: At SOC ≥140%, the anode becomes the dominant heat contributor (approximately 60%) during thermal runaway, due to heat released by reaction between deposited lithium and the electrolyte.
claims.quote: the heat contributed ratio from the anode became dominant (about 60%) at ≥140% SOC, which was attributed to a large amount of heat generated by the deposited lithium and the electrolyte.

claims.claim: At 165% SOC, the cathode crystal structure of Lix(Ni0.6Mn0.2Co0.2)O2 transitions from layered rhombohedral (R-3m) to disordered spinel (Fd-3m) phase at room temperature (25°C), without requiring elevated temperature.
claims.quote: the XRD pattern of the cathode material at 165% SOC shows a disordered spinel (Fd-3m) phase at 25°C (room temperature).

claims.claim: The activation energy of exothermic side reactions and the pre-exponential factor both decrease with increasing SOC in overcharged LIBs, indicating greater propensity for chemical reaction at higher SOC.
claims.quote: the values of Ea and A decreased with the increase of SOC, which indicated that the higher SOC LIB was more prone to chemical reactions and that the reaction speed would increase due to the collapse of the material structure and excessive Li precipitated during overcharging.

claims.claim: In commercial electrolyte (1 M LiPF6 in EC-DMC 1:1) with a lithium metal strip and no TRI additive, the maximum self-heat rate is 0.68 °C min−1 occurring at 177.6 °C.
claims.quote: In the case of electrolyte without any TRI additive, the maximum self-heat rate is 0.68 ◦C min−1, which occurs at T = 177.6 ◦C.

claims.claim: Addition of 5 wt.% TRI-013 to 1 M LiPF6 in EC-DMC (1:1) suppresses the exothermic reaction with lithium metal to beyond 300 °C, compared to 177 °C for the baseline electrolyte.
claims.quote: with addition of TRI-013, the exothermic reaction is effectively suppressed up to a temperature beyond 300 ◦C.

claims.claim: Addition of 5 wt.% TRI-311 shifts the onset of the exothermic peak with lithium metal from 177 °C to 203 °C, with a self-heat rate of 0.84 °C min−1.
claims.quote: the onset of the exothermic peak with additive TRI-311 is shifted to higher temperature (203 ◦C) with a self-heat rate of 0.84 ◦C min−1.

claims.claim: Adding 2 wt.% TRI-001 to 1 M LiPF6 in EC-DMC (1:1) reduces ionic conductivity from 1.04 × 10−2 S cm−1 to 7.45 × 10−3 S cm−1 at 25 °C.

DSC studies showed that the maximum heat flow for the major exotherm of a fully charged LiNi0.8Co0.2O2 cathode is reduced from 15.3 W g−1 (baseline) to 4.9, 7.2, and 5.7 W g−1 with 5 wt.% TRI-311, TRI-013, and TRI-001, respectively.
claims.quote: Maximum heat ﬂow (W g−1) Cathode: 1 M LiPF6 in EC-DMC (1:1) 15.3; With 5 wt.% TRI-311 4.9; With 5 wt.% TRI-013 7.2; With 5 wt.% TRI-001 5.7

claims.claim: The maximum closed vessel deflagration pressure for the synthesized li-ion battery thermal runaway gas mixture (NCA cells, 100% SOC) is 6.7 bar-g (97.2 psig).
claims.quote: The maximum closed vessel deflagration pressure for this mixture is 6.7 bar-g (97.2 psig).

claims.claim: The maximum burning velocity for the synthesized li-ion battery thermal runaway gas mixture is 59 cm/s.
claims.quote: The maximum burning velocity for the mixture is 59 cm/s.

claims.claim: The lower flammability limit (LFL) of the synthesized li-ion battery thermal runaway gas mixture is 10.8 v% and the upper flammability limit (UFL) is 50.3 v%.
claims.quote: The LFL for this mixture is 10.8 v% and the UFL is 50.3 v%.

claims.claim: In Installation Test 1 (no fire suppression), a prompt partial volume deflagration occurred 31 seconds after gas release from the first cell thermal runaway, producing enough pressure to deform the door closure mechanism and force open the container doors, but not enough to operate any deflagration vents.
claims.quote: In Test 1, a prompt partial volume deflagration occurred after 31 s of gas release from the first cell thermal runaway... The deflagration produced enough pressure to deform the door closure mechanism and force open the container doors, but not enough pressure to operate any of the deflagration vents.

claims.claim: In Installation Test 2 with Novec 1230 clean agent suppression, a backdraft deflagration occurred within 20 seconds of opening the container door approximately 2 hours and 9 minutes after the first thermal runaway, when pre-opening upper layer gas concentrations included 5.8 v% H2, 7.7 v% O2, 8.3 v% CO, and 9.6 v% CO2.
claims.quote: Within 20 s of the door opening, a backdraft deflagration occurred... Prior to opening the door, upper layer gas concentrations measured were 5.8 v% H2, 7.7 v%, O2, 8.3 v% CO, and 9.6 v% CO2.

claims.claim: Maintaining enclosure-average runaway gas concentration below 25% LFL is not feasible even with an exhaust flow rate of 50 volume changes per hour for a full 9-cell module runaway in the test container.
claims.quote: it is not feasible to maintain the concentration under 25% LFL even with an exhaust flow rate of 50 vol changes per hour.

claims.claim: For all four modeled ventilation cases involving a 9-cell module runaway in the test container, peak runaway gas concentration remained under 60% LFL, including the case with no ventilation (Q=0) which reached a peak of 51% LFL.
claims.quote: The corresponding peak gas concentrations, cmax, are: i) cmax = 51 %LFL, ii) cmax = 41 %LFL, iii) cmax = 27 %LFL, and iv) cmax = 29 %LFL. Thus, in all four cases, the peak runaway gas concentration is under 60% LFL for an entire 9-cell module runaway.

claims.claim: Analysis of the partial volume deflagration in Test 1 revealed it was caused by ignition of between 32 L and 84 L of runaway gas, significantly less than the 136–147 L generated at the time of the deflagration, because the remainder was at concentrations below the 10.8 v% LFL.
claims.quote: the deflagration was caused by the ignition of between 32 L (1.1 ft3) and 84 L (3.0 ft3) of runaway gas, significantly less than the 136 (4.8 ft3) to 147 (5.2 ft3) liters generated at the time of the deflagration. The rest of the gas produced by the cell runaway must have been at concentrations below the 10.8 v% LFL and did not contribute to the deflagration.

claims.claim: Actuation of a Novec 1230 total flooding clean agent system suppressed prompt ignition of battery gases but was followed by a delayed ignition 28 minutes and 32 seconds after agent discharge, indicating clean agent suppression does not eliminate the delayed deflagration hazard.
claims.quote: The battery gases did not ignite before the Novec 1230 discharged into the container. However, a delayed ignition occurred 28 min and 32 s after the clean agent discharge and resulted in fire.

claims.claim: Container door latch strength is estimated at 2–4 kPa (0.3–0.6 psig), while container walls and ceiling have an estimated Pes of 40–60 kPa (5.8–8.7 psig), and container deflagration pressures exceeding 70 kPa (10.2 psig) can cause doors to become dangerous projectiles.
claims.quote: the door latch strength is estimated to be in the range 2 kPa–4 kPa (0.3–0.6 psi-g)... The walls and ceiling of the container are estimated to have a Pes value of 40–60 kPa (5.8–8.7 psig)... Those tests also showed that container deflagration pressures exceeding 70 kPa (10.2 psig) can cause the doors to be blown off and become dangerous projectiles.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway threshold temperature T2 is 180–250°C, accompanied by separator collapse, dramatic release of oxygen from the cathode, and significant hydrogen production from the anode, with a temperature rise rate of 60–104°C/min.
claims.quote: the battery temperature reaches the thermal runaway threshold T2 (180–250 °C). Accompanied by several lethal triggers, including separator collapse, dramatic release of oxygen from the cathode, and significant hydrogen production from the anode, the battery enters an intense TR stage with a temperature rise rate of 60–104 °C/min.

claims.claim: In the thermal runaway process, heat generated by internal short circuit accounts for only 2% of total heat, while chemical exothermic reactions account for 98%.
claims.quote: According to a rough estimation, the heat generated by the internal short circuit is only 2 %, and the chemical exothermic reaction accounts for 98 %.

claims.claim: LiPF6 decomposes above 60°C to produce PF5, which reacts with organic solvents to produce HF and other reducing gases.
claims.quote: LiPF6 is sensitive to moisture and decomposes easily above 60 °C, where it can produce the strong Lewis acid phosphorus pentafluoride (PF5), and PF5 reacts with organic solvents to produce HF and other reducing gases.

claims.claim: Fluorides combined with NCM811 exhibit heat release below 200 J/g with onset/peak temperatures above 200°C, whereas TMP combined with a fully charged cathode results in significant heat release of 425.6 J/g.
claims.quote: The fluorides exhibited relatively lower heat release (< 200 J/g) and higher onset/peak temperatures (> 200 °C) in general. On the contrary, the methyl-containing TMP were not thermally stable enough when combined with a fully charged cathode, resulting in significant heat release (425.6 J/g).

claims.claim: Higher degree of alkyl fluorination in fluorinated phosphates (TDP, BMP, TFP) increases flame-retardant efficiency but also produces a thicker SEI layer and greater interfacial transfer impedance.
claims.quote: they found that the higher the degree of alkyl fluorination, the higher the flame-retardant efficiency... the fluorinated substitution of more branched chains, although is effective in improving the flame-retardant efficiency, brought about a thicker SEI layer and greater interfacial transfer impedance.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway typically occurs within 0.1 s, making it difficult for safety countermeasures to take effect during the TR event itself.
claims.quote: Considering that TR usually occurs within 0.1 s, it is difficult for safety countermeasures to take effect during TR.

claims.claim: An electrolyte with SET < 6 s/g is classified as non-combustible, SET > 20 s/g as flammable, and 6 s/g < SET < 20 s/g as flame-retardant.
claims.quote: They also divided the electrolyte into three categories: (1) SET < 6 s/g, non-combustible; (2) SET > 20 s/g, flammable; (3) 6 s/g < SET < 20 s/g, flame-retardant.

claims.claim: Al-anode ISC has a resistance of approximately 50 mΩ and is considered the most severe type of internal short circuit because the thermal conductivity of electrode material is small, causing 'hot spot' phenomenon.
claims.quote: Al-anode short is the most severe [3,5] because the resistance is little (∼50 mΩ) and the thermal conductivity of electrode material is small. Local heat is hard to conduct outside and 'hot spot' phenomenon occurs as a result.

claims.claim: Cathode-Cu and cathode-anode ISC have high contact resistance (≫100 mΩ), which limits short current density and local heating.
claims.quote: Cathode-Cu and cathode-anode ISC have high contact resistance (≫100 mΩ). As a result, the short current density is limited, as well as the local heating and local temperature.

claims.claim: Under nail penetration, all cell layers are short and internal short current is divided among all layers, whereas under one-layer ISC all internal short current flows through a single point, resulting in much higher short current at that point.
claims.quote: under nail penetration condition all layers are short and the internal short current is divided into all layers. But under one-layer ISC condition only one layer is short and the internal short current all flows through that internal short point. As a result, the short current through internal short point may be much high for one-layer Al-anode ISC.

claims.claim: In Al-anode ISC, the maximum temperature increases with increasing number of cell unit layers because more intact layers supply energy to the internal short resistance, increasing short current and Joule heat.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature, which occurs at the short circuit point, increases with adding of the layer number in the Al-anode ISC. The reason is that more intact cell unit layers can supply energy for the internal short resistance.

claims.claim: Forcing the battery to external short circuit when ISC is detected reduces internal short circuit current, and if the external short circuit resistance is small enough, the local temperature at the internal short point is within safe range.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature of battery B and C, suffering Al-anode ISC at SOC = 100%, decreases with the decreasing of external resistance... The local temperature of internal short point is within safe range if the external short circuit resistance is small enough.

claims.claim: A twofold increase in battery resistance lowers the maximum temperature during Al-anode ISC by approximately 40%.
claims.quote: A twofold increase in battery resistance can lower the maximum temperature by about 40%.

claims.claim: Conventional PP/PE/PP separator shutdown is activated only when the separator temperature reaches approximately 130 °C, providing very little protection from internal short circuit because only the separator near the short point reaches that temperature.
claims.quote: Conventional PP/PE/PP separator possessed a shutdown temperature at about 130 °C... thermal shutdown is activated only when the temperature of separator reaches the shutdown temperature... separator near the short point is melted while the distant part remain unchanged. That means the thermal shutdown can provide very little protection from an internal short circuit.

claims.claim: The optimal temperature range for lithium-ion battery operation is 20°C to 50°C with a temperature difference of less than 5°C to ensure battery safety.
claims.quote: It is imperative to maintain the battery pack within the optimal temperature range of 20◦C to 50◦C with a temperature difference (ΔT) of less than 5◦C to ensure battery safety.

claims.claim: At a maximum coolant flow rate of 0.12 m/s, the maximum Reynolds number for water in the spatial network flow channel system is 477.7, which is below the turbulent flow threshold of approximately 2300, confirming laminar flow.
claims.quote: when the maximum flow rate of the coolant is 0.12 m/s, the maximum Reynolds number for water is 477.7. This value is significantly lower than the commonly accepted threshold for turbulent flow (Reynolds number of approximately 2300). Therefore, it can be confirmed that the flow state of water at this flow rate is laminar.

claims.claim: When the number of secondary channels N increases from 2 to 4, Tmax decreases by 4.02°C and ΔT decreases by 3.84°C, but when N increases to 5, Tmax and ΔT only decrease by an additional 0.36°C and 0.42°C respectively, indicating diminishing returns.
claims.quote: When N is increased from 2 to 4, Tmax and ΔT decrease by 4.02◦C and 3.84◦C. However, when N is 5, Tmax and ΔT only decrease by 0.36◦C and 0.42◦C, respectively.

claims.claim: When coolant flow velocity increases from 0.04 m/s to 0.08 m/s, Tmax decreases from 44.98°C to 43.72°C and PCM liquid fraction decreases from 21.23% to 7.59%, but increasing velocity beyond 0.09 m/s yields only a 0.05°C further reduction in Tmax.
claims.quote: When V increases from 0.04 m/s to 0.08 m/s, the Tmax of the battery decreases from 44.98◦C to 43.72◦C, and the α of PCM decreases from 21.23 % to 7.59 %. However, when V is further increased to 0.09 m/s, Tmax is only reduced by 0.05◦C, and the α of PCM is also reduced by only 1.61 %.

claims.claim: PCM-only cooling systems risk cooling failure due to complete PCM melting during high-rate charge and discharge operations, because accumulated heat cannot be removed promptly.
claims.quote: it has also been observed that accumulated heat can be challenging to remove from the system in promptly when employing only PCM during charge and discharge operations at a high rate, potentially leading to cooling failure with the complete melting of PCM.

claims.claim: The proposed liquid-PCM hybrid BTMS with spatial network flow channels reduced maximum temperature by 20.13°C compared to a traditional solid aluminum heat sink at the same mass flow under 3C discharge.
claims.quote: The proposed BTMS controls the maximum temperature at 43.83◦C with a discharge rate of 3C, which is reduced by 20.13◦C compared to the traditional system with a solid aluminum heat sink at the same mass flow.

claims.claim: Segmenting PCM into multiple sub-domains using spatial network flow channels prevents liquefied PCM from flowing freely and reduces leakage risk.
claims.quote: Even if the PCM is liquified in these zones, the melted PCM is unable to flow freely because the lattice of spatial flow channels encloses the liquid PCM and effectively prevents the leakage of PCM.

claims.claim: The NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite pouch cell shows 83% capacity retention after 600 cycles at 1C under 45°C, compared to sharp attenuation of pristine NCM under the same conditions.
claims.quote: Under 45 ℃, the NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite pouch cell shows excellent 83% capacity retention after 600 cycles at 1C, which compared to the sharp attenuation of pristine NCM.

claims.claim: The thermal runaway temperature (TTR) of the NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite cell is delayed by 193°C compared to pristine NCM||graphite cell.
claims.quote: NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite cell could pass the nail penetration tests and the thermal runaway temperature (TTR) delayed by 193℃, which proved the double layer design has better safety performance.

claims.claim: External safety strategies involving coolants, phase-change heat absorbers, and extinguishants fail under extreme temperature and pressure due to the battery's own weight and bulk.
claims.quote: these external safety strategies would fail under extreme temperature and pressure, due to the battery's own weight and bulk.

claims.claim: The onset temperature (Tonset) of NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite cell is 90°C, which is higher than 85°C for pristine NCM||graphite cell, indicating more stable SEI film formed on the double layer cathode.
claims.quote: The onset temperature (Tonset) of NCM||graphite cell is 85 ℃, which is lower than 90 ℃ of NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite, indicating more stable SEI film formed on double layer cathode.

claims.claim: During nail penetration at 100% SOC, the pristine NCM||graphite cell combusted immediately upon puncture, while the NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite cell exhibited only voltage jitter and passed without fire.
claims.quote: The NCM||graphite cell shorted and quickly combusted at the moment of puncture. However, the NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite pouch cell just has voltage jitter, but safely pass detection with no fire evident.

claims.claim: The initial exothermic peak of pristine NCM cathode appears at 225°C, whereas the first exothermic peak of NCM/LFP (6:1) appears at 234°C with a much smaller enthalpy.
claims.quote: The initial exothermic peak of pristine NCM appears at 225℃, whereas the first exothermic peak of NCM/LFP (6:1) appears at 234℃ with a much smaller enthalpy, indicating that the double layer design enhances the thermal stability of the material.

claims.claim: Transition metal (Ni, Co, Mn) content deposited on the graphite anode is greater for pristine NCM||graphite cells than for NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite cells after 100 cycles.
claims.quote: We tested the TM content deposited on the graphite anode, and found that the TM content (Ni, Co, Mn) of NCM||graphite cell is more than TM content of NCM/LFP (6:1)||graphite.

claims.claim: The melting point of PP separator is approximately 165°C and PE separator is approximately 135°C.
claims.quote: The melting points (Tm) is just ∼165 for PP and ∼135°C for PE, respectively.

claims.claim: In PP/PE/PP trilayer separators, when internal battery temperature increases above approximately 130°C, the porous middle PE layer partially melts, closing pores and preventing lithium ion conduction while PP layers maintain dimensional stability.
claims.quote: When the internal temperature of the battery increases above ∼130°C, the porous middle PE layer partially melts, closing the pores inside the separator and preventing conduction of lithium ions in the liquid electrolytes, while the PP layer provides mechanical support to maintain the overall dimensional stability, thus avoiding internal short-circuiting.

claims.claim: Decomposition of unstable components in the SEI begins at roughly above 90°C during thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Big current causes the internal temperature to rise up quickly, which triggers the decomposition of unstable components in the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) (roughly >90°C).

claims.claim: LiCoO2 cathode decomposition starts at approximately 180°C, releasing gaseous oxygen.
claims.quote: the decomposition of the lithium metal oxide cathode material (Take LiCoO2 as an example, the decomposition starts at ∼180°C) and results in releasing of gaseous oxygen.

claims.claim: EC+DMC (1:1 by weight) electrolyte has a vapor pressure of 4.8 kPa at room temperature and an extremely low flash point of 25±1°C at 1.013 bar air pressure.
claims.quote: Take the ethylene carbonate (EC) + dimethyl carbonate (DMC) (1:1 by weight), as an example, it exhibits a vapor pressure of 4.8 kPa at room temperature and an extremely low flash point of 25±1°C at an air pressure of 1.013 bar.

claims.claim: Electrolyte containing just 5 wt% (ethoxy)pentafluorocyclotriphosphazene can totally suppress the flammability of conventional carbonate electrolyte with no impact on electrochemical performance of electrodes.
claims.quote: the electrolyte merely containing just 5 wt% (ethoxy)pentafluorocyclotriphosphazene can totally suppress the flammability of the conventional carbonate electrolyte with no impact on the electrochemical performance of electrodes.

claims.claim: In dilute salt electrolyte (1.0 M LiFSA in TMP), free TMP molecules co-intercalate and exfoliate graphite layered structure, but at high concentration (5.3 M LiFSA in TMP), most TMP molecules are coordinated with Li+ cations, forming a uniform and robust salt-derived inorganic SEI on the anode.
claims.quote: In dilute salt electrolyte, i.e., 1.0 M LiN(SO2F)2 (LiFSA) dissolved in TMP, the many free TMP molecules existed in the solution could co-intercalation and exfoliated the graphite layered structure. However, with a super-high concentration (e.g., 5.3 M LiFSA in TMP), most of the TMP molecules are coordinated with Li+ cations and the FSA− anion is more prone to decompose due to the modiﬁed LUMO structure of the peculiar solution structure unique to the concentrated electrolyte system.

claims.claim: The electrochemical stability window of water-in-salt electrolyte (LiTFSI at molality >20 M in water) is expanded to approximately 3.0 V, compared to the standard 1.23 V limit of aqueous electrolytes.
claims.quote: a 'water-in-salt' electrolyte is obtained by dissolving lithium bis(trifluoromethane sulfonyl)imide (LiTFSI) at a molality >20 M in water... The electrochemical stability window of this highly concentrated water-in-salt electrolyte is significantly expanded to ∼3.0 V.

claims.claim: Poly(benzyl methacrylate) (PBMA) dissolved in ionic liquid [EMIM][TFSI] phase separates above 100°C and causes almost complete cell shut-off above its LCST at 150°C due to increased internal resistance.
