claims.quote: At elevated temperatures, the PBMA phase separates above the phase transition temperature (>100°C), which acts as an electronic insulator with very low Li-ion conductivity, thus causing an increase in internal resistances at the electrode/electrolyte interface. Thus, almost complete shut-off occurs in cells as a result of an increase in internal resistance above the LCST (at 150°C).

claims.claim: The trilayer separator with silica nanoparticles sandwiched between two PE separators extended battery lifetime by approximately 5 times compared with conventional separators.
claims.quote: the life time of the battery with the trilayered separator was significantly extended by ∼5 times compared with that with conventional separators.

claims.claim: External PTC devices may fail to prevent thermal runaway because temperature increases inside cells may occur too fast to be detected.
claims.quote: temperature increases inside cells may occur too fast to be detected by the external PTC device and thermal runaway could still happen occasionally.

claims.claim: Cathodic stability limits of high-concentration aqueous electrolytes situate between 1.7 and 1.9 V versus Li, excluding high energy-density anode materials such as silicon, graphite, and Li metal.
claims.quote: the 'cathodic challenge' still exists, where the cathodic stability limits of these aqueous electrolytes all situate between 1.7 and 1.9 V versus Li, thereby excluding the most energy-dense anode materials such as silicon, graphite, and Li metal.

claims.claim: The theoretical oxidative dissolution potential of a Cu current collector in LIBs is 3.38 V (vs. Li/Li+), derived from the standard electrode potentials of Cu (+0.34 V vs. SHE) and Li (-3.04 V vs. SHE) at 298.15 K.
claims.quote: The standard electrode potential of Cu and Li is +0.34 V and −3.04 V (vs. SHE, 298.15 K), respectively. Therefore, the theoretical oxidative dissolution potential of a Cu current collector in LIBs is 3.38 V (vs. Li/Li+).

claims.claim: Due to kinetic limitations, the potential of the working cathode is located in the range of 3.4–3.6 V (vs. Li/Li+) during over-discharge, and a characteristic potential plateau of Cu oxidation at 3.56 V vs. Li/Li+ on the anode was observed when the cell was discharged to reverse voltage.
claims.quote: Due to kinetic limitations, the potential of working cathode located in the range of 3.4–3.6 V (vs. Li/Li+). Johannes et al. observed a characteristic potential plateau of Cu oxidation at 3.56 V vs. Li/Li+ on the anode when the cell was discharged to reverse voltage.

claims.claim: In LFP|Graphite cells, degradation and regrowth of the SEI film caused 24.46% capacity fading when over-discharged to 0.0 V in 110 cycles, while Cu dissolution was not found.
claims.quote: In the study of Zheng et al. with LFP | Graphite cells, the degradation and regrowth of SEI film results in 24.46% capacity fading when over-discharged to 0.0 V in 110 cycles, while Cu dissolution was not found.

claims.claim: At low C-rate during over-discharge cycling, anode side reactions such as SEI breakdown, Cu dissolution, and gas releasing are the dominant degradation factors, while at high C-rate, cathode degradation factors such as CEI layer evolution and electrolyte decomposition become dominant.
claims.quote: At low C-rate, anode side reactions such as SEI breakdown, Cu dissolution and gas releasing are the dominant factors, while at high C-rate, cathode degradation factors such as CEI layer evolution and electrolyte decomposition are gradually significant and become the dominant factors of battery degradation.

claims.claim: Adding Li2NiO2 (5 wt%) to LCO cathode limited the maximum anode voltage to below 3.4 V (vs. Li/Li+) during over-discharge, preventing Cu oxidative dissolution and maintaining almost 100% capacity retention rate after over-discharge to 0 V, compared to ~50% capacity loss after three cycles without Li2NiO2.
claims.quote: the anode potential of the battery without Li2NiO2 reached a plateau ∼3.6 V (vs Li/Li+), indicating severe Cu oxidative dissolution, with a capacity loss of about 50% after three cycles of discharge to 0 V. But for cells containing Li2NiO2, the max anode voltage was limited to below 3.4 V (vs Li/Li+), which was below the initiation potential of Cu oxidation, thereby maintaining almost 100% CRR after over-discharge to 0 V.

claims.claim: Adding 8 wt% of Li6CoO4 to LCO cathode achieved the best capacity retention rate for both aged and fresh batteries during zero-volt storage when the electrode potential range was kept below 2.0 V (vs. Li/Li+), while higher or lower potential ranges induced capacity loss.
claims.quote: Adding 8 wt% of Li6CoO4 exhibited the best CRR for both aged and fresh batteries with the electrode potential range below 2.0 V (vs. Li/Li+), while the higher or lower potential ranges induces capacity loss.

claims.claim: For a LCO|MCMB battery with Ti current collectors, capacity loss due to SEI degradation and regrowth increased at higher storage potentials during zero-volt storage.
claims.quote: The MCMB anode with Ti current collectors were stored at different potentials. The capacity loss due to SEI degradation and regrowth became more at higher storage potentials.

claims.claim: An LCO|LTO battery had almost no capacity loss after multiple 7-day zero-volt storage cycles, while an LCO|graphite battery lost almost all capacity after five 7-day zero-volt storage cycles with rapid increases in internal resistance and battery thickness.
claims.quote: The LCO | LTO battery had almost no capacity loss, while the LCO | graphite battery lost almost all capacity after five 7-day zero-volt storage cycles, with a rapid increase in internal resistance and battery thickness.

claims.claim: When the temperature of a lithium-ion battery reaches about 80 °C, self-heating starts due to decomposition of the solid electrolyte interface (SEI) on the anode material surface.
claims.quote: when the temperature of LIB reaches about 80 °C, the self-heating of LIB starts by decomposition of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) on the anode material surface

claims.claim: The reaction between anode material and electrolyte occurs at about 130 °C, and the reaction between anode material and binder occurs at a higher temperature.
claims.quote: the reaction occurs between the anode material and electrolyte at about 130 °C, and between the anode material and binder at a higher temperature

claims.claim: Layered-structure cathode materials including NCM111, NCA, and NCM811 release oxygen from the crystal structure at around 200 °C due to phase transition, which may oxidize the electrolyte or react with the anode material leading to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the layered-structure of cathode materials, such as NCM111, NCA, and NCM811, releases oxygen in the crystal structure at around 200 °C due to the phase transition. The released oxygen may oxidize the electrolyte or react with the anode material, leading to thermal runaway

claims.claim: At a heating rate of 0.9 °C min−1 around 200 °C, single-crystal particles NCM811/NG exhibit an exothermic peak of about 200 mW g−1, whereas polycrystalline particles NCM811/NG exhibit about 250 mW g−1.
claims.quote: At the heating rate of 0.9 °C min−1 around 200 °C, single-crystal particles NCM811/NG are doublets and about 200 mW g−1, whereas polycrystalline particles NCM811/NG are close to singlets and about 250 mW g−1

claims.claim: The average calorific value of single-crystal particles NCM811/NG and polycrystalline particles NCM811/NG from 50 °C to 325 °C is nearly identical (709 J g−1 vs 708 J g−1), but their reaction progress α differs.
claims.quote: Calculation of average calorific value from peak areas of single-crystal particles NCM811/NG and polycrystalline particles NCM811/NG from 50 °C to 325 °C under all heating conditions results in 709 J g−1 for single-crystal particles NCM811/NG and 708 J g−1 for polycrystalline particles NCM811/NG. These results clarify that the average calorific value of single-crystal particles NCM811/NG and polycrystalline particles NCM811/NG is almost the same value, but that reaction progress α differs

claims.claim: For the 12.5 Ah prismatic cell using polycrystalline particles NCM811/NG (cell D), the UN38 thermal test criteria are not met, with a thermal safety margin deficit of 26.5 h, whereas the cell using single-crystal particles NCM811/NG (cell C) is predicted to meet UN38 criteria.
claims.quote: In the case of 1 (UN38), cell D is not predicted to meet the criteria due to a lack of thermal safety margin of 26.5 h. On the other hand, cell C is predicted to meet the criteria of 1 (UN38)

claims.claim: If the heating rate is lower than 0.3 °C min−1, self-discharge will occur at lower temperatures due to the longer heating time, causing Li ions in the negative electrode to return to the positive electrode and increasing thermal stability of the cathode material.
claims.quote: If the heating rate is lower than 0.3 °C min−1, self-discharge will occur at lower temperatures due to the longer heating time. As a result, Li ions in the negative electrode return to the positive electrode, and the thermal stability of the cathode material increases

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in lithium-ion batteries can reach a critical point at temperatures as low as 160°C where the rate of heat generation outpaces the rate of heat loss.
claims.quote: this positive feedback loop can reach a critical point at temperatures as low as 160 ◦C where the rate of heat generation out paces the rate of heat loss, leading to catastrophic and sometimes explosive failures of lithium-based cells.

claims.claim: SEI decomposition on graphite anodes occurs around 69°C, with some reports at temperatures as low as 57°C.
claims.quote: SEI decomposition occurs at the lowest temperatures. This tends to happen around 69 ◦C, with some reports at temperatures as low as 57 ◦C.

claims.claim: Above 250°C, the graphite anode structure collapses and the SEI layer is no longer maintained, after which the electrolyte reacts with the electrode and releases flammable hydrocarbon gases.
claims.quote: Should temperatures continue to increase above 250 ◦C, the structure of the graphite anode collapses and the SEI is no longer maintained. After the breakdown of the SEI layer, the electrolyte reacts with the electrode and releases flammable hydrocarbon gases.

claims.claim: For NMC cathodes the onset of thermal runaway is around 170°C, while for LFP cathodes it is near 246°C.
claims.quote: For lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide (NMC) cathodes, the onset of thermal runaway is around 170 ◦C, whilst lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cathodes can expect temperatures near to 246 ◦C.

claims.claim: Polyethylene (PE) separators melt around 130°C, closing pores and preventing ion transfer, but if temperature continues to rise the separator shrinks and collapses, allowing electrode contact and internal short circuit.
claims.quote: Polyethylene (PE) is a commonly used polymer separator that typically melts around 130 ◦C. At this point, the holes within the separator will close making ion transfer difficult... Should the temperature further increase, the separator shrinks and collapses allowing the two electrodes to contact leading to an internal short circuit.

claims.claim: PAN membrane separators exhibit a PTCR effect beyond 80°C with resistance changes reported from around 10 Ω to around 100 Ω over a 10°C temperature increase, and the shutdown effect is reversible.
claims.quote: As the temperatures increase beyond 80 ◦C the polymer expands, cutting the ionic pathways resulting in a PTCR of several orders of magnitude... Changes in resistance have been reported from around 10 Ω to around 100 Ω over a 10 ◦C temperature increase... The result is a shutdown effect that is also reversible.

claims.claim: Ceramic coatings of Al2O3 and SiO2 on PP separators enhance the collapse temperature to between 200–260°C compared to the uncoated PP melt temperature of 170°C.
claims.quote: The collapse temperature of PP may be enhanced using ceramic coatings of Al2O3 and SiO2, giving reported temperatures between 200–260 ◦C.

claims.claim: Gel polymer electrolytes tend to have limited operating temperature ranges up to 90°C but improved room temperature conductivities compared to solid polymer electrolytes.
claims.quote: Gel polymers tend to have limited operating temperature ranges up to 90 ◦C but improved room temperature conductivities.

claims.claim: The PP/PE/PP trilayer separator shutdown effect is irreversible once it occurs, rendering the cell inoperable.
claims.quote: Unfortunately, once the shutdown effect has occurred the melt is irreversible and the cell becomes inoperable.

claims.claim: In conductive polymer binder composites, if the proportion of conductive additive is too high, the effect of thermal expansion on resistivity is reduced.
claims.quote: There is a percolation threshold for the conductive additive... Should too much additive be present, the effect of the thermal expansion on resistivity would be reduced.

claims.claim: Under low-oxygen (inert nitrogen) conditions, SIB and NCM cells exhibit over 35% decrease in gas generation metrics during thermal runaway, while LFP cells remain largely unaffected.
claims.quote: under low-oxygen conditions, SIB and nickel–cobalt–manganese (NCM) cells exhibit substantial mitigation of thermal runaway severity, including over 35 % decrease in gas generation metrics, while lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cells remain largely unaffected.

claims.claim: Under air atmosphere, peak temperatures during thermal runaway increase in the order LFP (526 ± 10°C) < SIB (700 ± 46.5°C) < NCM (857.5 ± 42.5°C).
claims.quote: the peak temperatures during thermal runaway increased in the order of LFP (526 ± 10 ◦C), SIB (700 ± 46.5 ◦C), and NCM (857.5 ± 42.5 ◦C).

claims.claim: Under inert atmosphere, the SIB cell's maximum pressure rise rate decreased by 96%, total gas volume decreased by 36.4%, and average gas generation rate decreased by 39.4% compared to air atmosphere.
claims.quote: its maximum pressure rise rate decreased by 96 %, total gas volume decreased by 36.4 %, and average gas generation rate decreased by 39.4 %, all representing significant reductions.

claims.claim: Under air atmosphere, both SIB and NCM cells showed nearly 50% reduction in oxygen concentration within the vessel during thermal runaway, while LFP exhibited no significant oxygen consumption.
claims.quote: both the SIB and NCM cells showed nearly a 50 % reduction in oxygen concentration within the vessel, suggesting that external oxygen was actively consumed during their thermal runaway processes... the LFP cell exhibited no significant oxygen consumption during thermal runaway.

claims.claim: LFP cells exhibited the highest hydrogen content in gas products under both air (56.3%) and inert (51.3%) atmospheres, resulting in the highest explosion risk index (ERL) among the three battery types.
claims.quote: LFP cells exhibited the highest hydrogen content under both atmospheres (56.3 % and 51.3 %), contributing significantly to their higher explosion risk... the three battery types followed the trend: LFP (air/N2) > NCM (air/N2) > SIB (air/N2).

claims.claim: NCM cells show significant decreases in CO2/CO and O2/N2 ratios under inert atmosphere compared to air, whereas SIB and LFP display no notable compositional changes.
claims.quote: NCM cells show significant decreases in CO2/CO and O2/N2 ratios, whereas SIB and LFP display no notable compositional changes.

claims.claim: Gas toxicity (FED) ranking across battery types under both atmospheres follows LFP < SIB < NCM, driven primarily by CO and CO2 concentrations.
claims.quote: The resulting FED values followed the order: LFP (air/N2) < SIB (air/N2) < NCM (air/N2). It is also noteworthy... Therefore, the concentrations of CO and CO2 are the primary determinants of gas toxicity.

claims.claim: In LCO cells with a shutdown separator, the separator shuts down at 120°C and breaks down at 160°C; once breakdown occurs, cathode and anode connect, resulting in cathode-anode type Internal Short Circuit.
claims.quote: The separator will shut down at 120°C and break down at 160°C. Once breakdown occurs, cell cathode and anode connects, resulting in cathode-anode type Internal Short Circuit (ISC).

claims.claim: With one-sided cooling and an aluminum heat spreader (k=200 W/m-K baseline), thermal runaway occurs at 11.6-C constant-current discharge; increasing TMS to 800 W/m-K raises this threshold to 14.6-C.
claims.quote: the numerical results show thermal runaway occurs at the constant-current discharge 11.6-C for the aluminum heat spreader baseline. With a 800 W/m-K TMS, a higher C-rate of 14.6-C is required before the module enters thermal runaway.

claims.claim: Three-sided cooling with an aluminum heat spreader (k=200 W/m-K) can safely dissipate up to 18-C discharge rates, while an 800 W/m-K TMS sustains rates to 26-C under three-sided cooling.
claims.quote: three-sided cooling with an aluminum heat spreader can safely dissipate up to 18-C discharge rates, while an 800 W/m-K heat spreader can sustain rates to 26-C.

claims.claim: Extremely aggressive internal short circuits (ISR ≤ 2 mΩ) are self-limiting with respect to fratricide because the shorted region rapidly reaches temperatures exceeding 700°C, forming electrochemically dead volumes that stop heat generation before propagation to adjacent cells occurs.
claims.quote: When ISR is 2 mΩ, the areas of cathode-anode short circuit at Cell 2 stop growing after 6 seconds, and does not negatively affect Cell 1... regions of Cell 2 reach temperatures that melt the aluminum current collector within the first 2 seconds, and this initial short zone becomes electrically non-conductive due to the high temperatures (> 700ºC). This non-conductive zone significantly reduces the subsequent amount of abuse heat generated.

claims.claim: Insulating layers (2 ≤ k ≤ 200 W/m-K) placed between cells delay but do not prevent heat propagation and do not offer significant cell fratricide protection in the absence of an effective heat-removal TMS.
claims.quote: insulating layers do not offer significant cell fratricide protection. High-conductance TMSs provide equal or better fratricide protection because heat is removed from the system. Insulating layers, on the other hand, delay the propagation of heat within the pack but do not prevent heat propagation, which ultimately results in fratricide in the absence of an effective TMS.

claims.claim: The limited thermal conductivity of Li-ion electrolytes prevents high-performance TMSs from stopping thermal runaway in source cells where an internal fault generates excessive abuse heat.
claims.quote: The limited thermal conductivity of Li-ion electrolytes prevents high performance TMSs from stopping thermal runaway in source cells, where an internal fault such as an internal short circuit generates excessive abuse heat.

claims.claim: Effective in-plane thermal conductivities around 2,000 W/m-K have the potential to eliminate cell fratricide for all but the most extreme thermal runaway scenarios.
claims.quote: effective in-plane conductivities around 2,000 W/m-K have the potential to eliminate fratricide for all but the most extreme runaway scenarios.

claims.claim: The polyethylene separator softens and melts at temperatures reaching 320–350 K, losing its physical integrity and allowing direct contact between cathode and anode, causing internal short circuits.
claims.quote: As temperature reaches to 320–350 K the polyethylene separator softens and melts & losing its physical integrity. This allow the direct contact between cathode and anode which causing internal short circuits that sharply increase localized current density and Joule heating (heat generation spikes from ~500 to >12,000 W/m3)

claims.claim: Cathode materials such as LiCoO₂ and NMC begin to release lattice oxygen at temperatures above 350 K, which reacts violently with electrolyte solvents producing exothermic oxidation and flammable gases including CO and CO₂.
claims.quote: the cathode materials like layered LiCoO₂, NMC begin to release lattice oxygen at temperatures >350 K... This oxygen reacts violently with electrolyte solvents, producing exothermic oxidation and generating heat and flammable gases like CO, CO₂, hydrocarbons.

claims.claim: Above 450 K, cathode material undergoes rapid decomposition releasing large amounts of oxygen that sustains combustion, with heat generation reaching up to 100,000 W/m³.
claims.quote: Above 450 K the cathode material undergo a rapid decompositions & releasing large amount of oxygen which sustains combustion of electrolyte vapors & decomposition product. Heat generation can reach up to 100,000 W/m3

claims.claim: At the catastrophic failure stage, temperatures exceed the melting point of aluminum current collectors (~933 K) and heat release rates exceed 100 kW per battery pack.
claims.quote: At this stage the temperatures exceeds melting point of aluminum current collector (~933 K) and other structural material. Complete collapse of cell & module internal architecture occurs. Large scale fire and explosion ensue with heat release rates exceeding 100 kW per battery pack.

claims.claim: In a worst-case two-wheeler scenario (4C discharge, 0.15 Ω internal resistance, 60°C initial temperature), hybrid cooling combining air, liquid, and PCM with 45 W cooling power is the only strategy that maintains battery temperature below the 90°C thermal runaway threshold, achieving ~40°C final temperature, while air cooling, liquid cooling, and PCM cooling alone each result in final temperatures of 128°C, 94.7°C, and 128°C respectively.
claims.quote: Hybrid Cooling: Cooling Power 45 W, Net Heat -19.8 W, ΔT -132°C, Final Temp Tmax ~40°C, Safe (≤ 90°C)? Yes... Air Cooling: Final Temp 128°C, Safe No. Liquid Cooling: Final Temp 94.7°C, Safe No (marginal). PCM Cooling: Final Temp 128°C, Safe No.

claims.claim: Lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cathodes provide superior thermal stability and are less prone to oxygen release compared to high energy density cathodes like LiCoO₂ and NMC, making LFP a safer choice for applications where safety is paramount.
claims.quote: Lithium iron phosphate cathodes while slightly lower in energy density & provide superior thermal stability are less prone to oxygen release & resist thermal runaway. This makes LFP a better safer choice for applications where safety is paramount like electric buses and stationary storages

claims.claim: Thermal runaway-induced battery fires can persist for extended periods, smouldering internally and reigniting hours or days after initial suppression, unlike conventional fuel fires.
claims.quote: Unlike conventional fuel fires the thermal runaway induced battery fires can persist for an extended period, smouldering internally & also reigniting hours or days after initial suppression.

claims.claim: A thickened SEI layer reduces battery capacity and efficiency, and when temperatures rise sufficiently the SEI can decompose, releasing heat and gases that can trigger thermal runaway.
claims.quote: A thickened SEI reduce the battery capacity and efficiency which can lead to overheating. When temperatures rise too high the SEI layer can break down & decompose, releasing heat and gases that can trigger thermal runaway

claims.claim: Overcharging lithium-ion cells beyond their upper voltage limit causes lithium ions to plate on the anode surface as metallic lithium instead of intercalating into graphite, forming dendrites that can penetrate the separator and cause internal short circuits.
claims.quote: Overcharging lithium-ion cell beyond their upper voltage limit destabilizes anode and cathode chemistry. Excessive voltage causes the lithium ion to plate on anode surface as metallic lithium instead of intercalating into graphite. This lithium plating forms dendrites needle like crystalline structure which can penetrate separator and cause internal short circuit

claims.claim: Charging at temperatures below freezing or above 45°C can accelerate battery degradation and dendrite formation.
claims.quote: Charging at temperature below the freezing or above 45°C can accelerate the degradation and dendrite formation.

claims.claim: In P2-type Na0.67Ni0.33Mn0.67O2 cathode material, when the charging voltage exceeds 4.2 V, the P2-O2 phase transition is initiated, leading to significant volume change and severe structural degradation.
claims.quote: When the charging voltage exceeds 4.2 V, the P2-O2 phase transition is initiated (Figure 4a). This phenomenon leads to a significant change in the material's volume and causes severe structural degradation (Figure 4b).

claims.claim: Sodium clusters formed in hard carbon anode under high state of charge have an electronic state density higher than metallic sodium, causing early linear carbonate decomposition of the electrolyte with self-exothermic reaction onset temperature as low as 92 °C.
claims.quote: sodium clusters form under a high state of charge (SOC), with an electronic state density higher than that of metallic sodium, leading to the early linear carbonate decomposition of the electrolyte and the self-exothermic reaction onset temperature as low as 92 °C.

claims.claim: 26650 cylindrical SIBs with CFM cathode and HC anode exhibit an initial decomposition temperature above 110 °C and a maximum thermal runaway temperature below 350 °C, demonstrating superior thermal stability compared to LIBs.
claims.quote: in comparison to LIBs, NIBs exhibit a higher initial decomposition temperature (>110 °C) and a lower maximum TR temperature (<350 °C), thereby highlighting their superior thermal stability.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in SIBs with NVOPF cathode is initiated at temperatures above 190 °C under a heating rate of 5 °C min−1, which is higher than the TR initiation temperature of NMC-based batteries under the same conditions.
claims.quote: TR was initiated at a relatively higher temperatures (above 190 °C) compared to NMC under a heating rate of 5 °C min−1.

claims.claim: Among commercial SIB cathode types, Na-NFM had the highest energy density (127 Wh kg−1) but significant TR risk, Na-tmCN had the lowest energy density (23 Wh kg−1) but best safety with self-heating rate below 1 °C min−1, and Na-PVF had medium energy density (47 Wh kg−1) with relatively good safety.
claims.quote: Na-NMF had the highest energy density (127 Wh kg−1), but a significant risk of TR, Na-tmCN had the lowest energy density (23 Wh kg−1), but the best cycling stability and safety, showing only a low self-heating rate (<1 °C min−1) in ARC and nail penetration tests, Na-PVF had a medium energy density (47 Wh kg−1) and relatively good safety.

claims.claim: Fe-PBA cathode materials contain crystal water, interstitial water, and adsorbed water due to [Fe(CN)6]4− vacancies, which escape into the electrolyte during high-voltage cycling and produce gaseous by-products including H2O, NH3, and CH4 that can lead to thermal runaway.
claims.quote: The vacancy formed will be occupied by water molecules, leading to the presence of crystal water, interstitial water, and adsorbed water, which then escape into the electrolyte during high-voltage cycling. Side reactions occur and form gaseous by-products, such as H2O, ammonia (NH3), and methane (CH4), which can easily lead to TR.

claims.claim: At low temperatures, slow Na+ migration kinetics in SIB electrolytes cause Na+ to deposit on the anode surface as metallic sodium, promoting dendrite growth and potential short circuits.
claims.quote: At low temperatures, slow reaction kinetics also lead to severe polarization of electrochemical reactions. Na+ tends to deposit on the surface of the anode material, forming metallic sodium and promoting the growth of sodium dendrites, which may induce safety issues such as battery short circuits.

claims.claim: The thermal stability of charged NVP/C cathode materials decreases with increasing state of charge.
claims.quote: This indicates that the thermal stability of charged NVP/C materials follows a decreasing order with increasing SOC.

claims.claim: PP/PE/PP trilayer separators fail to prevent thermal runaway because the melting point of PE (130 °C) is only 30 °C lower than the melting temperature of PP (160 °C), allowing thermal inertia after battery shutdown to cause separator shrinkage and internal short-circuit.
claims.quote: This is because the melting point of PE (130 °C) is only 30 °C lower than the melting temperature of PP (160 °C). Thermal inertia after battery shutdown can easily cause the internal temperature of the batteries to reach the melting point of PP, leading to the separator shrinking and causing an internal short-circuit.

claims.claim: Cells with thermal shutdown separators can remain inactive for as little as three minutes before failing due to internal short circuits caused by thermal inertia.
claims.quote: In certain instances, cells with thermal shutdown separators remain inactive for as little as three minutes before failing due to internal short circuits.

claims.claim: The LiTFSI0.15DMFu0.8VC1.0 electrolyte exhibits ionic conductivity of 1.18 mS cm‒1 at room temperature and a lithium-ion transference number of 0.58, which is higher than that of conventional LP30 electrolyte (0.46).
claims.quote: The LiTFSI0.15DMFu0.8VC1.0 electrolyte shows a high ionic conductivity of 1.18 mS cm‒1 at room temperature. The lithium-ion transference number of the LiTFSI0.15DMFu0.8VC1.0 electrolyte is estimated to be higher than (tLi+ = 0.58) the conventional LP30 electrolyte (tLi+ = 0.46).

claims.claim: At 120 °C, the ionic conductivity of the LiTFSI0.15DMFu0.8VC1.0 electrolyte decreases by two orders of magnitude to 1.37 × 10‒5 S cm‒1, attributed to increased viscosity and reduced lithium salt solubility from Diels-Alder oligomer formation.
claims.quote: the LiTFSI0.15DMFu0.8VC1.0 electrolyte exhibited an ionic conductivity of 1.37 × 10‒5 at 120 °C... The substantial decrease in ionic conductivity of the LiTFSI0.15DMFu0.8VC1.0 electrolyte by two orders of magnitude can be ascribed to the increased sample viscosity and reduced lithium salt solubility.

claims.claim: At 120 °C, the electrolyte viscosity surged to 4.2 × 10³ mPa·s within 10 minutes and plateaued at 7.2 × 10⁷ mPa·s after 230 minutes, whereas at 100 °C the viscosity increased more gradually to 2.7 × 10³ mPa·s in 10 minutes.
claims.quote: At 120 °C, the electrolyte viscosity surged sharply (4.2 × 103 mPa.s in 10 min), indicating rapid Diels-Alder reactions and plateaued at 7.2 × 107 mPa·s after 230 min due to increased molecular weight. In contrast, at 100 °C, the viscosity increased gradually (2.7 × 103 mPa·s in 10 min), initiating a warning phase.

claims.claim: Cells with pristine PP/PE/PP separators and LP30 electrolyte experienced OCP drop to 0 V within 40 minutes at 150 °C and within 25 minutes at 170 °C due to separator thermal shrinkage causing internal short circuit, while cells with Diels-Alder oligomer-modified separators maintained OCP for over 70 minutes at both temperatures.
claims.quote: For cells with pristine separators, the OCP dropped to 0 V within 40 min at 150 °C and within 25 min at 170 °C, likely due to thermal shrinkage of the separator, causing an internal short circuit. In contrast, cells with oligomers-modiﬁed separators maintained their OCP for over 70 min at both temperatures.

claims.claim: The graphite||LiFePO4 cell with LiTFSI0.15DMFu0.8VC1.0 electrolyte showed specific capacity drop from 158 mA h g‒1 at 80 °C to 45 mA h g‒1 at 100 °C, and complete thermal shutdown at 120 °C.
claims.quote: At 80 °C, the speciﬁc capacity slightly increased to 158 mA h g−1... However, the speciﬁc capacity dropped signiﬁcantly to 45 mAh g−1 at 100 °C, signaling a gradual suppression of electrochemical activity as an early warning mechanism. The cell exhibited a strict thermal shutdown behavior when the temperature was further raised to 120 °C.

claims.claim: At 5C discharge rate and 40°C ambient temperature, liquid cooling alone results in Tmax of 69.21°C, while PCM cooling alone gives 54.70°C and composite PCM/water cooling gives 54.53°C.
claims.quote: At the discharge rate of 5C, the Tmax of battery pack cooled by water reaches 69.21°C, and the PCM cooling and composite PCM and water cooling just are 54.70°C and 54.53°C respectively, which is much lower than the water cooling.

claims.claim: With liquid cooling only, the maximum battery temperature exceeds the safety limit of 55°C when the discharge rate exceeds 3C at 40°C ambient temperature.
claims.quote: the Tmax exceeds the safety limit of 55°C when the discharge rate exceeds 3C

claims.claim: When EG mass fraction in CPCM is below 16%, increasing EG content decreases Tmax but increases ΔTmax; once EG mass fraction reaches 16% and continues to increase, both Tmax and ΔTmax increase because latent heat decreases too much.
claims.quote: when mass fraction of EG is lower than 16%, the Tmax of battery pack is decreased with the increase of EG, but the ΔTmax is increased. Once the mass fraction of EG reaches 16% and continues to increase, the ΔTmax is increased along with the increase of Tmax. In this situation, the latent heat of CPCM decreases too much, and the CPCM near batteries is incapable to absorb all the heat released by the battery pack

claims.claim: Fin scheme 1, where fins directly contact batteries and water channels, reduces Tmax from 54.53°C to 53.39°C but increases ΔTmax from 2.86°C to 9.08°C due to excessively low local temperature at fin-battery contact points.
claims.quote: After arranging the fins, the Tmax is decreased from 54.53°C to 53.39°C, but the ΔTmax is increased from 2.86°C to 9.08°C. The reduction in temperature field of battery pack shows a large nonuniformity. Mainly due to the large thermal conductivity of the Al fins, the temperature at the place of direct contact between the batteries and the fins is excessively low.

claims.claim: To maintain ΔTmax within 5°C, the coolant inlet temperature must not be lower than the ambient temperature when ambient temperature is below 23.5°C, whereas at 40°C ambient temperature the minimum inlet temperature can be approximately 28°C.
claims.quote: To maintain the ΔTmax within 5°C, the inlet temperature must not be lower than the ambient temperature when the ambient temperature is lower than 23.5°C. At the high ambient temperature of 40°C, the minimum of inlet temperature can be about 28°C

claims.claim: When battery temperature exceeds approximately 80 °C, the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) undergoes repeated decomposition and reformation, while exposed active lithium engages in continuous exothermic reaction with the electrolyte.
claims.quote: When the temperature exceeds ≈80 °C, the solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) undergoes repeated decomposition and reformation, while the exposed active lithium—comprising lithiated graphite and lithium metal—engages in a continuous exothermic reaction with the electrolyte.

claims.claim: The separator collapses at approximately 130–170 °C, triggering complete internal short circuit (ISC) in the middle stage of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: In the middle stage, the separator collapses at ≈130—170 °C, triggering complete ISC.

claims.claim: The heat capacity of the paraffin-based separator (88.7 J g−1) is significantly lower than the heat released by the reaction of lithiated graphite with electrolyte (439 J g−1), limiting its effectiveness in suppressing thermal runaway under sustained high temperatures or overcharging.
claims.quote: the heat capacity of the paraﬃn-based separator is only 88.7 J g−1, signiﬁcantly lower than the heat released by the reaction of lithiated graphite/electrolyte (439 J g−1). Consequently, its eﬀectiveness in suppressing TR under sustained high temperatures or overcharging may be limited.

claims.claim: Even in the absence of a short circuit, a battery assembled with thermally stable PET-ceramic nonwoven separators will still undergo thermal runaway triggered by Joule heat, because oxygen generated from the cathode at elevated temperature shuttles through the separator to the anode and reacts violently with lithiated graphite.
claims.quote: It was found that even in the absence of a short circuit, the battery would still undergo TR triggered by Joule heat. Speciﬁcally, the oxygen generated from the cathode at elevated temperature will shuttle through the separator to the anode, where it will react with lithiated graphite in a violent redox reaction, generating a large amount of heat and thus triggering TR.

claims.claim: ARC tests on 1 Ah NCM622/Gr cells showed maximum self-heating rates of 1944.5 °C s−1 for PE separator, 702.5 °C s−1 for Al2O3@PE separator, and 8.5 °C s−1 for the bi-continuous nanoporous separator (Bi-Sep).
claims.quote: ARC tests on 1 Ah NCM622/Gr cells with PE, Al2O3@PE, and Bi-Sep reveals the maximum self-heating rates of the cells of 1944.5, 702.5, and 8.5 °C s−1, respectively.

claims.claim: Resolving the flammability of electrolytes alone cannot completely prevent the occurrence of thermal runaway in lithium batteries.
claims.quote: recent studies have shown that even if the ﬂammability of electrolytes is resolved, it still cannot completely prevent the occurrence of TR.

claims.claim: In conventional PP/PE/PP separators, the melting points of both PP (≈165 °C) and PE (≈135 °C) layers are close, so thermal inertia can cause the cell temperature to rise to the melting point of PP, leading to severe separator contraction and potential short circuit.
claims.quote: since the melting points of both layers are close, the cell temperature will easily rise to the melting temperature of PP due to thermal inertia, leading to the severe contraction of the separator and potentially causing a short circuit. Consequently, conventional PP/PE/PP separators are often only suitable for abuse situations where the battery temperature increases slowly.

claims.claim: In 1 Ah NMC811|Gr pouch cells, the thermal runaway trigger temperature (T2) of FPECell was 168.2 °C, compared to 126.5 °C for TraCell, representing an increase of +41.7 °C.
claims.quote: the trigger temperature (T2) (168.2 °C) of the FPECell was much higher than that of the TraCell (T2 = 126.5 °C), which greatly increased the thermal runaway threshold

claims.claim: In 1 Ah NMC811|Gr pouch cells, the maximum thermal runaway temperature (T3) of FPECell was 522.9 °C, compared to 728.6 °C for TraCell, a reduction of 205.7 °C.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature of thermal runaway (T3) was located at 728.6 °C due to severe side reactions between the reactive NMC811 and the conventional electrolyte... T3 of the battery with 1 Ah FPEele based cell (FPECell) was only 522.9 °C, which was 205.7 °C lower than that of the TraCell

claims.claim: In an 8-cell module thermal propagation simulation with FPEele, thermal runaway in cell 1 was triggered at 165 s but none of the remaining seven cells underwent thermal runaway.
claims.quote: the thermal runaway in cell1 with FPEele was triggered at 165 s. Surprisingly, none of the eight cells with the FPEele was triggered

claims.claim: A PP separator soaked with FPEele was quenched within 2 s of direct flame ignition, whereas a separator soaked with Traele was almost completely burned out after 8 s.
claims.quote: After burning for 8 s, the separator was almost completely burned out. For the separator soaked with FPEele, the flame diminished rapidly and was quenched within 2 s

claims.claim: FPECell retained 83.2% capacity after 500 cycles at 1/3 C, whereas TraCell retained only 48.3% capacity after 480 cycles under the same conditions.
claims.quote: the capacity still approached 922 mAh after 500 cycles, exhibiting a high capacity retention (83.2%)... capacity was decreased to 535.2 mAh after 480 cycles (capacity retention rate = 48.3%)

claims.claim: The FPE additive mechanism for flame retardancy involves free radicals released upon heating that capture hydrogen or oxyhydrogen free radicals in the gas phase, blocking the combustion chain reaction.
claims.quote: when the flame retardant was heated, it released free radicals. The free radicals captured hydrogen free radicals or oxyhydrogen free radicals in the gas phase, blocked the chain reaction, and made the organic electrolyte difficult to burn

claims.claim: The ionic conductivity of FPEele (1 M LiPF6/EC+EMC+DMC+FPE) was 8.80 mS cm⁻¹ at 30 °C, compared to 7.91 mS cm⁻¹ for Traele (1 M LiPF6/EC+EMC+DMC) at the same temperature.
claims.quote: Traele... Conductivity [ms cm−1] at 30 oC: 7.91; FPEele... 8.80

claims.claim: Thermal runaway in LIBs begins when the battery temperature rises above approximately 80 °C, causing an increase in exothermic chemical reaction rates that further heats the cell in a positive feedback cycle.
claims.quote: As the temperature of the battery rises to above ≈80 °C, the exothermic chemical reaction rate inside the batteries increases and further heats up the cell, resulting in a positive feedback cycle.

claims.claim: Adding large amounts of TRRs directly to electrolytes degrades overall ionic conductivity, resulting in poor electrochemical performance.
claims.quote: in most cases, huge amounts of TRRs are required to ensure a high level of cell safety, which consequently degrades the overall ionic conductivity of the electrolyte, resulting in poor electrochemical performance.

claims.claim: Cell specific capacity decreased by approximately 42% after 50 cycles when 5 wt% TCP was added directly to the electrolyte, whereas with the same quantity of TCP@PUF microcapsules the cycle specific capacity hardly changed.
claims.quote: The cell speciﬁc capacity decreased by ≈42% after 50 cycles after adding 5% TCP directly to the electrolyte, compared to electrolyte without TCP. In contrast, with the same quantity of TCP@PUF microcapsules, the cell cycle speciﬁc capacity hardly changed.

claims.claim: For TPP@PVDF-HFP integrated capsule separators soaked in EC/DEC electrolyte at room temperature (~25 °C), only ~4% of encapsulated TPP was released, but upon heating to 160 °C all (~100%) of the TPP was abruptly released.
claims.quote: When the TPP@PVDF-HFP ﬁber was soaked in the EC/diethyl carbonate (DEC) electrolyte and stored at room temperature (≈25 °C), the release of TPP into the electrolyte was only ≈4%. However, upon heating up to 160 °C, above the melting points of TPP (≈50 °C) and PVDF-HFP (≈150 °C), all of the encapsulated TPP ≈100%) was abruptly released into the electrolyte.

claims.claim: In a nail penetration test, cells with DMTP@PMMA microcapsule-coated PE separator reached a maximum temperature of 37.2 °C, compared to 72.3 °C for pristine PE separator cells and 56.2 °C for PMMA-coated PE separator cells.
claims.quote: In the nail penetration test, the highest temperatures of the cells using a pristine PE separator, a PMMA-coated PE separator, and a DMTP@PMMA microcapsule-coated PE separator were 72.3 °C, 56.2 °C, and 37.2 °C, respectively.

claims.claim: For PI–TPP@Cu/Al integrated capsule current collectors, mismatched thermal expansion coefficients of PI and Cu/Al cause cracking during thermal runaway, releasing TPP; at temperatures above 244 °C (TPP boiling point), the flame retardant is gasified and quickly released.
claims.quote: the mismatched temperature coeﬃcients of PI and Cu/Al will result in breakage of the original planar structure and cause cracks during TR, which will release TPP or TPP radicals. At temperatures above 244 °C (the boiling point of TPP), the ﬂame retardant will be gasiﬁed and quickly released.

claims.claim: Boehmite (AlOOH) decomposes endothermically at approximately 400 °C, forming Al2O3 and releasing water, which lowers temperature and dilutes combustible gases, making it applicable to suppressing the third stage of thermal runaway.
claims.quote: Because the decomposition temperature of boehmite is ≈400 °C, it can be used to suppress the third stage of the TR process

claims.claim: When DOPO microcapsule content was increased from 10% to 15%, safety was hardly improved but electrochemical performance was reduced by approximately 15%.
claims.quote: when the amount of DOPO microcapsules is increased from 10% to 15%, the safety is hardly improved, but the electrochemical performance is reduced by ≈15%.

claims.claim: At a discharge rate of 8C, the spiderweb-fin-enhanced PCM-based BTMS reduced the maximum battery temperature from 57.7°C to 42.1°C, a decrease of 27.1%.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature of the battery pack is reduced from the initial 57.7 C with a decrease of 27.1 %. C to 42.1

claims.claim: At a fin thickness of 2 mm, the battery temperature reached 50.008°C, exceeding the optimal operating temperature range for lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: As the thickness of the fins is 2 mm, the battery temperature has already reached 50.008 C, which exceeds the optimal temperature range for the operation of lithium-ion batteries.

claims.claim: Increasing fin thickness from 2 mm to 8 mm caused the temperature difference to increase from 0.6458°C to 1.8079°C, because thicker fins create a greater temperature difference between the battery surface and interior.
claims.quote: as the fin thickness increases from 2 mm to 8 mm, the corresponding temperature difference increases from 0.6458 C to 1.8079 C. This is because the thicker the fins, the greater the temperature difference between the surface and interior of the battery, resulting in an enormous overall temperature difference.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature of the battery exceeded the ideal operating temperature range of 20°C–50°C at a discharge rate of 7C without a cooling system.
claims.quote: the maximum temperature has exceeded the ideal operating temperature of LIBs (20 C) at 7C. The maximum temperature reaches 57.6 C when the battery is operating at 8C.

claims.claim: The PCM-based BTMS with plain fins provided the same cooling effect as a pure PCM system, while the spiderweb-fin BTMS outperformed both by controlling maximum temperature at 42.1°C versus 45.1°C for pure PCM.
claims.quote: In the PCM-based BTMS with plain fins, the temperature growth curve of the battery almost coincides with that of the pure PCM system, and the cooling effect is the same. The PCM-based BTMS with spiderweb-shaped fins performs best among the four systems. The maximum temperature of the battery is controlled at 42.1 C with a decline of 27.1 %.

claims.claim: The spiderweb-fin PCM BTMS performance is limited relative to liquid-cooled coupled PCM BTMS because it relies on a single cooling method where heat dissipation depends mainly on PCM heat absorption and convective exchange between fins and air.
claims.quote: there is a particular gap in cooling efficiency between the spiderweb fins-enhanced PCM-BTMS and the liquid-cooled-coupled PCM-based BTMS...the BTMS proposed in this article has two limitations: Firstly, only a single cooling method is adopted, and the system's heat dissipation mainly depends on the PCM's heat absorption, as well as the convective heat exchange between the fins and the air.

claims.claim: PCM with expandable graphite (PCM-nf) inhibits combustion flame and lowers peak battery temperature during thermal runaway, but accelerates thermal runaway propagation speed.
claims.quote: the PCM added with expandable graphite considerably inhibits the combustion ﬂame and lowers the peak temperature of battery modules; however, it accelerates the thermal runaway propagation speed.

claims.claim: PCM-nf reduces peak battery temperature from 528.9 °C to 417.9 °C during thermal runaway in a 2×2 18650 NMC module at 75% SOC.
claims.quote: the PCM-nf reduces the peak temperature from 528.9 to 417.9 °C (Fig. 10A(a) and 10A(b)).

claims.claim: PCM-nf shortens thermal runaway propagation phase II interval from 183 s to 64 s compared to the reference group, indicating accelerated propagation.
claims.quote: PCM-nf considerably shortens phase II from 183 s to 64 s.

claims.claim: Aerogel felt delays initial thermal runaway trigger time from 573 s to 826 s and extends phase II propagation duration from 183 s to 257 s compared to the reference group.
claims.quote: the aerogel not only defers the initial TR time from 573 s to 826 s but also delays the propagation speed by extending the duration of phase II from 183 to 257 s.

claims.claim: Aerogel felt exhibits worse heat dissipation than PCM during cycling, causing higher battery temperature and greater temperature inhomogeneity than the blank reference group.
claims.quote: The aerogel exhibits a slight eﬀect on heat dissipation and even causes higher battery temperature... the use of aerogel even aggravates the temperature inhomogeneity among the batteries compared to the temperature state of the reference group.

claims.claim: The optimized module combining PCM-nf and aerogel felt delays initial thermal runaway by 173 s and reduces peak temperature from 528.9 °C to 385.0 °C compared to the reference group.
claims.quote: the TR is delayed for approximately 173 s in the optimized module. The peak temperatures of the batteries in the optimized module is also reduced from 528.9 to 385.0 °C.

claims.claim: The optimized module with PCM-nf and aerogel felt shows less than 1 °C difference in maximum temperature and less than 0.2 °C difference in temperature uniformity compared to a PCM-only module during cycling tests.
claims.quote: The temperature variation of the optimized structure exhibits a slight diﬀerence from that of the structure with PCM; their diﬀerence is less than 1 °C... For ΔTmax(t), the diﬀerence between these two structures is less than 0.2 °C.

claims.claim: Traditional PCM (without flame retardant) aggravates thermal runaway failure propagation among battery modules.
claims.quote: Ouyang et al. [30] conﬁrmed that the traditional PCM aggravates the failure propagation among the battery modules after studying the eﬀects of the use of traditional PCM during TR tests.

claims.claim: CPCM with 40 wt.% paraffin and 60 wt.% silica aerogel achieves a thermal conductivity of 0.051 W/(m·K) at a density of 424 kg/m3.
claims.quote: The CPCM with 40 wt.% paraffin (PA) and 60 wt.% silica aerogel (SA) achieves the lowest thermal conductivity of 0.051 W/(m•K) at the density of 424 kg/m3.

claims.claim: In a battery module without FR-CPCM thermal barrier, thermal runaway propagated to the adjacent battery in only 63 seconds after the triggered battery reached a maximum temperature of 582°C.
claims.quote: For the module without thermal barrier, TR propagates to its neighbor by only 63 s after it is triggered, even though the maximum temperature of the triggered battery is 582°C.

claims.claim: With 3 mm FR-CPCM-4 plates between batteries, TR propagation was prevented and the maximum external surface temperature of the adjacent battery was kept below 182.6°C.
claims.quote: After the triggered battery went to TR, highest external surface temperature of the adjacent battery was effectively controlled on less than 182.6°C. While TR in module without FR-CPCM-4 propagated to its adjacent one in only 63 s.

claims.claim: Samples with SA mass ratio of 40% or 30% (CPCM-1 and CPCM-2) were excluded from consideration because thermal conductivity higher than 0.1 W/(m·K) is not helpful to prevent thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: While samples with 40% and 30% SA (CPCM-1 and CPCM-2) were not taken into consideration in the following discussion, due to thermal conductivity higher than 0.1 W/(m•K) is not helpful to prevent the thermal runaway propagation.

claims.claim: The flame retardant coating converts CPCM from a not-classified (NC) flammability rating to V-0, with LOI values of 58.25% and 56.31% for FR-CPCM-3 and FR-CPCM-4 respectively, due to formation of a dense carbon layer by carbonization of APP and DEPR.
claims.quote: The carbonization reaction of APP and DEPR in the coating forms a dense carbon layer at the bottom part of the sample. And the carbon layer prevents the direct contact between flame and the internal PA, reduces the diffusion of oxygen to the interior of FR-CPCM, and slows down the heat transfer from flame to the inner part which avoids the vaporization of PA, thus improving the flame retardant properties of FR-CPCM.

claims.claim: FR-CPCM-4 achieved a maximum temperature difference (ΔT) of 428°C at a bottom surface temperature of 563°C, compared to a maximum ΔT of 263°C for aerogel blanket at a bottom surface temperature of 648°C, indicating superior thermal insulation performance.
claims.quote: For the FR-CPCM-4 sample, the highest ∆T is 428°C when Tb is 563°C. while a highest ∆T of 263°C is obtained for aerogel blanket at the Tb of 648°C.

claims.claim: Annealed Ti3C2Tz MXene anode delayed thermal runaway onset of a single 18650 battery by 43 seconds compared to a graphite anode under 200 W heating and 100% SOC.
claims.quote: ATM anode delays TR by 43 s

claims.claim: The TR trigger temperature for the ATM-anode battery was 496 K, compared to 482 K for the graphite-anode battery, under identical 200 W heating conditions at 100% SOC.
claims.quote: At 395 s and 437 s, the temperature curves related to graphite and ATM anodes increase sharply, indicating occurrence of TR and corresponding to 482 K and 496 K trigger temperatures, respectively.

claims.claim: When spacing between cells exceeded 0.5 mm, only batteries neighboring the heater (batteries 2 and 5) experienced thermal runaway, while all batteries suffered TR when spacing was 0 mm, regardless of anode material.
claims.quote: In Case 1 (δ = 0 mm), all batteries suffer TR, while in Cases 2–4 where δ gets larger than 0.5 mm, only batteries 2 and 5 experience TR due to limited transferred heat to the remaining batteries.

claims.claim: For 25% and 50% SOC, replacing graphite anode with ATM anode had little effect on TR propagation in the battery pack, whereas appreciable differences in peak temperature and onset time were observed at 75% and 100% SOC.
claims.quote: For 25% and 50% SOCs, the two curves corresponding to the two types of batteries almost overlap each other, indicating changing anode material has little effect on TR propagation in battery packs. As SOC increases to 75% and 100%, appreciable discrepancy can be noticed between the two sets of curves.

claims.claim: The peak temperature increasing rate (dT/dt) of the ATM-anode battery 2 remained approximately invariant as heating power increased from 100 W to 400 W, whereas the peak dT/dt of the graphite-anode battery increased substantially over the same range.
claims.quote: As P increases from 100 W to 400 W, the peak value of dT/dt related to ATM anode barely changes, suggesting the ATM-based anode battery shows better thermal stability. Whereas the peak value of dT/dt related to graphite anode increases greatly.

claims.claim: The critical SOC for triggering TR propagation to battery 5 (second-neighbor battery) in the ATM-anode pack with 1 mm spacing and 200 W heating is between 50% and 75% SOC.
claims.quote: These results indicate the critical SOC for trigging TR of battery 5 is located between 50% and 75% SOC.

claims.claim: The threshold temperature for battery thermal runaway is 120°C, and a BTMS is required to maintain battery temperature below this value to prevent fire under extreme conditions.
claims.quote: a battery thermal management system (BTMS) is required to maintain the battery temperature below 120°C, which is the threshold temperature for thermal runaway, to prevent the risk of fire under extreme conditions

claims.claim: Li-ion batteries require a BTMS to maintain temperature below 50°C during normal operations to avoid capacity loss.
claims.quote: Li-ion batteries also require a BTMS to maintain the battery temperature below 50°C during normal operations to avoid problems such as capacity loss

claims.claim: SAT-urea/EG undergoes solid-liquid phase change at 50.3°C with a latent heat storage density of 181 kJ/kg, and thermal decomposition at 114.0°C with a thermochemical storage density of 567.3 kJ/kg.
claims.quote: Sodium acetate trihydrate-urea/expanded graphite—an inorganic salt hydrate—undergoes a solid-liquid phase change at 50.3°C, with a corresponding latent heat storage density of 181 kJ/kg, and thermal decomposition at 114.0°C, with a corresponding thermochemical storage density of 567.3 kJ/kg.

claims.claim: The latent heat densities (~150 kJ/kg) of previously studied PCMs are far too low to store the large amount of heat (~880 kJ/kg) released during battery thermal runaway, and once the latent heat is exhausted, the high thermal conductivity of the PCMs would accelerate thermal runaway propagation.
claims.quote: The latent heat densities (~150 kJ/kg) of previously studied PCMs are far too low to store the large amount of heat (~880 kJ/kg) released during battery thermal runaway. Once the latent heat is exhausted, the high thermal conductivity of the PCMs would accelerate thermal runaway propagation

claims.claim: A mini-channel liquid cooling system requires a flow rate of 10 L/min per battery to prevent thermal runaway propagation; for a car with 80 batteries this totals 2400 L/min, which exceeds the maximum flow rate in an electric vehicle cooling system of 200 L/min.
claims.quote: Ma revealed that a mini-channel liquid cooling system requires a flow rate of 10 L/min for each battery to prevent thermal runaway propagation. For a car with 80 batteries, such a system would require a flow rate of 2400 L/min, which is far beyond the maximum flow rate in an electric vehicle cooling system (200 L/min)

claims.claim: Most previously studied PCMs for battery thermal management are flammable organics, and burning of the PCM could introduce additional heat into the battery system, further intensifying thermal runaway.
claims.quote: most of them are flammable organics with a high risk of combustion and explosion. The burning of the PCM could introduce additional heat into the battery system, further intensifying thermal runaway.

claims.claim: The total thermal storage density of SAT-urea/EG is approximately 1000 kJ/kg, comprising latent heat storage of 181 kJ/kg, thermochemical storage of 567 kJ/kg, and sensible heat storage of 300 kJ/kg from 25°C to 120°C, which is nearly two times that of paraffin/EG.
claims.quote: The total thermal storage density of SAT-urea/EG was approximately 1000 kJ/kg, with a latent heat storage of 181 kJ/kg, thermochemical storage of 567 kJ/kg, and sensible heat storage of 300 kJ/kg (from 25°C to 120°C). The thermal storage density of SAT-urea/EG was nearly two times that of the paraffin/EG

claims.claim: Without thermochemical storage, an increase of at least 242°C was observed in the cell under thermal runaway and thermal runaway propagation was triggered in adjacent cells, with adjacent cell temperatures reaching ~700°C at 60 s.
claims.quote: Without the thermochemical storage process, an increase of at least 242°C was observed in the cell under thermal runaway and thermal runaway was triggered in the adjacent cells later. At 60 s, the temperature of adjacent cells increases to ~700°C while the temperature of the triggered cell reached to ~500°C.
